{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27336","id":2709384517,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs6hfe1F","number":27336,"title":"'Xor' is printed as 'a ^ b', but is recognized by Sympy as Power: Xor(a, b) -> (a ^ b) -> (a**b)","user":{"login":"ducktalez","id":47595755,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ3NTk1NzU1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/47595755?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez","html_url":"https://github.com/ducktalez","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ducktalez/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-01T15:33:17Z","updated_at":"2024-12-02T06:20:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I wanted to use xor-expresions in sympify, but it did not work.

Not really a bug, but I realized I could not load `Xor` with `a ^ b` in sympify, as it is interpreted as `a**b`.

Is this intentional? 

```
import sympy

print(sympy.sympify('Xor(a, b)'))
print(sympy.sympify('a ^ b'))
print(sympy.sympify('a**b'))
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2510673332","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27336#issuecomment-2510673332","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336","id":2510673332,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6VpdW0","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-02T06:20:50Z","updated_at":"2024-12-02T06:20:50Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I doubt it's intentional, but at the same time, it's hard to change at this point (it would be a breaking change). Do note however, that using `sympify` to create an expression from a string is discouraged. Try to use `parse_expr` instead:
```python
>>> srepr(parse_expr("a^b"))
"Xor(Symbol('a'), Symbol('b'))"
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2510673332/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Fixes #24922

Brief description of what is fixed or changed

This PR resolves partial issue https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/24922 by improving the code quality from using ".func is" to "isinstance()" in tests. 

Other comments

### Changes Made:
- Replaced `.func is` with `isinstance()` in relevant places in the codebase.

Release Notes

* core
   * Replaced `.func is` with `isinstance()` in relevant places in the codebase.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

wanted to use xor-expresions in sympify, but it did not work.

Not really a bug, but I realized I could not load `Xor` with `a ^ b` in sympify, as it is interpreted as `a**b`.

Is this intentional? 

```
import sympy

print(sympy.sympify('Xor(a, b)'))
print(sympy.sympify('a ^ b'))
print(sympy.sympify('a**b'))
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2520327406","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27336#issuecomment-2520327406","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27336","id":2520327406,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6WOSTu","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-05T13:27:50Z","updated_at":"2024-12-05T13:27:50Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think the situation is more nuanced than "caret being standard ascii representation" (see this excellent answer for example https://softwareengineering.stackexchange.com/a/331392/300252).

The [documentation](https://docs.sympy.org/latest/modules/core.html#module-sympy.core.sympify) of `sympify` clearly states that it parses the text as python syntax, and that the user should use `parse_expr` when deviating from python compatible syntax.

>To parse non-Python syntax, use parse_expr from sympy.parsing.sympy_parser

I think it could be valuable to add an example using `^` to the docstring of sympify, since the treatment of this character clearly deviates from this rule (and the keyword argument is confusingly named `convert_xor` rather than "caret_is_exponentiation").","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2520327406/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"closed","number":27350,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/pulls/27350","id":2221138709,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAAnGFs6EY-MV","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/27350","diff_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/27350.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/27350.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27350","number":27350,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Add type hints for `Mul(*terms)` and related methods.","user":{"login":"oscarbenjamin","id":1159732,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNTk3MzI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1159732?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin","html_url":"https://github.com/oscarbenjamin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/oscarbenjamin/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"<!-- Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

I asked about this in the Python [typing forum](https://discuss.python.org/t/change-type-signature-for-an-inherited-method-implementation/73076). Using a `TYPE_CHECKING` block in subclasses is the best way to communicate that a subclass returns different types.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Adds hints for e.g. `Add(*terms)` and many other basic things.

#### Other comments


#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

I asked about this in the Python [typing forum](https://discuss.python.org/t/change-type-signature-for-an-inherited-method-implementation/73076). Using a `TYPE_CHECKING` block in subclasses is the best way to communicate that a subclass returns different types.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Adds hints for e.g. `Add(*terms)` and many other basic things.

was previous discussion about this in the PR gh-23181 (CC @eagleoflqj). I also have a related PR gh-27353.

The `symbols` function is impossible to use for downstream code that wants to use static typing. There are three basic problems that prevent it from working properly:

Firstly symbols returns different types depending on the value (rather than the type) of the string argument:
```python
In [1]: type(symbols('x, y'))
Out[1]: tuple

In [2]: type(symbols('x'))
Out[2]: sympy.core.symbol.Symbol
```
This makes it impossible to infer the types in the most common usage of the `symbols` function. The reason this is done is so that you can do:
```python
x = symbols('x')
x, y = symbols('x, y')
```
Otherwise you would need something like this for a single symbol:
```python
[x] = symbols('x')
x, = symbols('x')
```
Of course there is no reason why you need to use `symbols` for a single symbol but that usage is widespread including in the sympy codebase itself.

The second problem with symbols is that it can be used to create other types e.g.:
```python
f, g, h = symbols('f, g, h', cls=Function)
```
In principle this should be understandable to a type checker and I can write type annotations that pyright will understand but I have not succeeded in getting anything that mypy will understand if there is a default value `cls=Symbol`. Trying to make this work would be quite disruptive to users because it would end up meaning that they get errors from mypy any time they use the symbols function.

The third problem is just that it does all kinds of weird stuff like:
```python
In [7]: symbols(['x:3', 'y', 'z', [[['Q:2:2', 'z']]]])
Out[7]: [(x₀, x₁, x₂), y, z, [[[(Q₀₀, Q₀₁, Q₁₀, Q₁₁), z]]]]
```

In the PR gh-27353 I have added an overload that says that when using the parameter `seq=True` the return type will always be a tuple of Symbol:
```python
x, y, z = symbols('x, y, z', seq=True)
```
That makes is possible for both pyright and mypy to infer the types and can be used in downstream codebases that depend on sympy since it works with older sympy versions and can be understood by a type checker. I also used that in the sympy.abc module so that the symbols there have inferred types.

Using `seq=True` like this solves problem 1 because the type is unambiguous but it ignores problem 2 by assuming that it is always Symbol and it also ignores the weird types in problem three but I think that is okay. So this isn't ideal but I think it is good as a starting point that there is at least some well-typed way of calling `symbols` for the most common usage.

The main problems with the `seq=True` approach are:

1. I don't think we really want users to have to write `seq=True` although I think some will be happy to use it if it makes type checking possible.
2. It still assumes that it is always `Symbol` so e.g. `symbols('f, g, h', cls=Function)` would be interpreted incorrectly by a type checker.

I think that the proper solution is just that we need a new function that is suitable for anyone downstream to create symbols of different types in a statically typed context. I have a demonstration here of what I have done with the overloads in gh-27353 as well as adding a new function called `symbols_type` which can be called like `symbols_type('x, y, z', Function)`:
```python
from typing import (
    reveal_type,
    TypeVar,
    Callable,
    Any,
    ParamSpec,
    Concatenate,
    overload,
    Literal,
)

from collections.abc import Iterable
from sympy import Expr, Symbol, Function, MatrixSymbol

@overload
def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq: Literal[False] = False,
    **kwargs: bool | int,) -> Any: ...
@overload
def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq: Literal[True],
    **kwargs: bool | int,) -> tuple[Symbol, ...]: ...

def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq=True, **kwargs) -> Any | tuple[Symbol, ...]:
    syms = tuple(cls(name, **kwargs) for name in names.split(", "))
    if not seq and len(syms) == 1:
        return syms[0]
    else:
        return syms


T = TypeVar("T")
P = ParamSpec("P")


def symbols_type(
    names: str | Iterable[str | tuple[str, int] | tuple[str, int, int]],
    func: Callable[Concatenate[str, P], T],
    *args: P.args,
    **kwargs: P.kwargs,
) -> tuple[T, ...]:
    """Make symbols of type T.

    >>> symbols_type('x, y, z', Symbol)
    (x, y, z)
    >>> symbols_type(['x', 'y', 'z'], Symbol)
    (x, y, z)

    Symbols of different types can be made:

    >>> symbols_type('f, g, h', Function)
    (f, g, h)
    >>> symbols_type('A, B, C', MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)
    (A, B, C)

    Keyword arguments such as assumptions for Symbol can be passed:

    >>> symbols_type('a, b, c', Symbol, integer=True)
    (a, b, c)

    Multiple symbols can be generated with numbered names:

    >>> symbols_type([('x', 3), ('y', 2)], Symbol)
    (x0, x1, x2, y0, y1)
    >>> symbols_type([('x', 3, 6)], Symbol)
    (x3, x4, x5)
    """

    match names:
        case str():
            names_list = [n.strip() for n in names.split(",") for n in n.split()]
        case Iterable():
            names_list = [n for item in names for n in _parse_item(item)]
        case _:
            raise TypeError("Expecting str or iterable")

    return tuple(func(name, *args, **kwargs) for name in names_list)


def _parse_item(item: str | tuple[str, int] | tuple[str, int, int]) -> list[str]:
    """Handle e.g. ('x', 3) -> ['x0', 'x1', 'x2']"""
    match item:
        case str(name):
            return [name]
        case (str(name), int(stop)):
            return [name + str(i) for i in range(stop)]
        case (str(name), int(start), int(stop)):
            return [name + str(i) for i in range(start, stop)]
        case _:
            raise TypeError("Expected str, (str,int), or (str,int,int)")


a, b, c = symbols("a, b, c")
x, y, z = symbols("x, y, z", seq=True)
i, j, k = symbols_type("i, j, k", Symbol)
f, g, h = symbols_type("f, g, h", Function)
A, B, C = symbols_type("A, B, C", MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)

reveal_type(a)  # Any
reveal_type(x)  # Symbol
reveal_type(i)  # Symbol
reveal_type(f)  # type[AppliedUndef]
reveal_type(f(x))  # Expr
reveal_type(A)  # MatrixSymbol


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import doctest

    doctest.testmod()
```
My suggestion would be to leave the existing `symbols` function as is apart from adding hints for `seq=True` and then add a new function suitable for downstream use and also that should be mostly used throughout the sympy codebase. Here I have suggested calling the function `symbols_type` because the idea is that you pass the type of symbol that you want as the second argument. Other names like `syms` etc could be used.

Using positional arguments for the type means we can pass additional positional arguments to the symbol constructor e.g.:
```python
In [2]: A, B, C = symbols_type("A, B, C", MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)

In [3]: A.shape
Out[3]: (2, 2)
```
This is something that needs a `lambda` with `symbols`:
```python
In [5]: A, B, C = symbols("A, B, C", cls=lambda n: MatrixSymbol(n, 2, 2))
```
I don't think that it is possible with `symbols_type` to have a default argument for the type i.e. to have it default to `Symbol` without complaints from mypy. Maybe it is possible and someone else can figure that out but it is really just a deficiency in mypy because there are many ways to get pyright to understand this. For now though we could add something like `symbols_type` that has a non-optional second argument so to use it with Symbol it would be `symbols_type('x, y, z', Symbol)` or perhaps `syms('x, y, z', Symbol)`.

The implementation that I showed allows creating multiple symbols using range syntax like `symbols_type([('x', 10)], Symbol)`. I think that is better than `symbols('x:10')`  because often I want to create `n` symbols where `n` is a Python variable and it is awkward to format that into a string. I don't think that it is worth supporting any of the other cases that `symbols` handles. ultimately in more complicated cases you should just use a loop or something because the purpose of symbols is just to be convenient for the common case where all the names are spelled out.

Obviously there are many possibilities for what the name could be and what sort of options and arguments it should take but I think that many users are going to want a function that works with type checkers so we need to add from typing import (
    reveal_type,
    TypeVar,
    Callable,
    Any,
    ParamSpec,
    Concatenate,
    overload,
    Literal,
)

from collections.abc import Iterable
from sympy import Expr, Symbol, Function, MatrixSymbol

@overload
def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq: Literal[False] = False,
    **kwargs: bool | int,) -> Any: ...
@overload
def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq: Literal[True],
    **kwargs: bool | int,) -> tuple[Symbol, ...]: ...

def symbols(names: str, *, cls: Any = Symbol, seq=True, **kwargs) -> Any | tuple[Symbol, ...]:
    syms = tuple(cls(name, **kwargs) for name in names.split(", "))
    if not seq and len(syms) == 1:
        return syms[0]
    else:
        return syms


T = TypeVar("T")
P = ParamSpec("P")


def symbols_type(
    names: str | Iterable[str | tuple[str, int] | tuple[str, int, int]],
    func: Callable[Concatenate[str, P], T],
    *args: P.args,
    **kwargs: P.kwargs,
) -> tuple[T, ...]:
    """Make symbols of type T.

    >>> symbols_type('x, y, z', Symbol)
    (x, y, z)
    >>> symbols_type(['x', 'y', 'z'], Symbol)
    (x, y, z)

    Symbols of different types can be made:

    >>> symbols_type('f, g, h', Function)
    (f, g, h)
    >>> symbols_type('A, B, C', MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)
    (A, B, C)

    Keyword arguments such as assumptions for Symbol can be passed:

    >>> symbols_type('a, b, c', Symbol, integer=True)
    (a, b, c)

    Multiple symbols can be generated with numbered names:

    >>> symbols_type([('x', 3), ('y', 2)], Symbol)
    (x0, x1, x2, y0, y1)
    >>> symbols_type([('x', 3, 6)], Symbol)
    (x3, x4, x5)
    """

    match names:
        case str():
            names_list = [n.strip() for n in names.split(",") for n in n.split()]
        case Iterable():
            names_list = [n for item in names for n in _parse_item(item)]
        case _:
            raise TypeError("Expecting str or iterable")

    return tuple(func(name, *args, **kwargs) for name in names_list)


def _parse_item(item: str | tuple[str, int] | tuple[str, int, int]) -> list[str]:
    """Handle e.g. ('x', 3) -> ['x0', 'x1', 'x2']"""
    match item:
        case str(name):
            return [name]
        case (str(name), int(stop)):
            return [name + str(i) for i in range(stop)]
        case (str(name), int(start), int(stop)):
            return [name + str(i) for i in range(start, stop)]
        case _:
            raise TypeError("Expected str, (str,int), or (str,int,int)")

x, y, z = symbols("x, y, z", seq=True)
i, j, k = symbols_type("i, j, k", Symbol)
f, g, h = symbols_type("f, g, h", Function)
A, B, C = symbols_type("A, B, C", MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)

reveal_type(a)  # Any
reveal_type(x)  # Symbol
reveal_type(i)  # Symbol
reveal_type(f)  # type[AppliedUndef]
reveal_type(f(x))  # Expr
reveal_type(A)  # MatrixSymbol


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import doctest

    doctest.testmod()
```
My suggestion would be to leave the existing `symbols` function as is apart from adding hints for `seq=True` and then add a new function suitable for downstream use and also that should be mostly used throughout the sympy codebase. Here I have suggested calling the function `symbols_type` because the idea is that you pass the type of symbol that you want as the second argument. Other names like `syms` etc could be used.

Using positional arguments for the type means we can pass additional positional arguments to the symbol constructor e.g.:
```python
In [2]: A, B, C = symbols_type("A, B, C", MatrixSymbol, 2, 2)

In [3]: A.shape
Out[3]: (2, 2)
```
This is something that needs a `lambda` with `symbols`:
```python
In [5]: A, B, C = symbols("A, B, C", cls=lambda n: MatrixSymbol(n, 2, 2))
```
I don't think that it is possible with `symbols_type` to have a default argument for the type i.e. to have it default to `Symbol` without complaints from mypy. Maybe it is possible and someone else can figure that out but it is really just a deficiency in mypy because there are many ways to get pyright to understand this. For now though we could add something like `symbols_type` that has a non-optional second argument so to use it with Symbol it would be `symbols_type('x, y, z', Symbol)` or perhaps `syms('x, y, z', Symbol)`.

The implementation that I showed allows creating multiple symbols using range syntax like `symbols_type([('x', 10)], Symbol)`. I think that is better than `symbols('x:10')`  because often I want to create `n` symbols where `n` is a Python variable and it is awkward to format that into a string. I don't think that it is worth supporting any of the other cases that `symbols` handles. ultimately in more complicated cases you should just use a loop or something because the purpose of symbols is just to be convenient for the common case where all the names are spelled out.

Obviously there are many possibilities for what the name could be and what sort of options and arguments it should take but I think that many users are going to want a function that works with type checkers so we need to add something.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27354/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27354/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2530408368","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27354#issuecomment-2530408368","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27354","id":2530408368,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6W0vew","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-10T05:07:07Z","updated_at":"2024-12-10T05:07:07Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> ... minimise the chance of an error:

If an expression completely factorizes, it's enough to call `factor` on it:
```python
>>> factor(x**2 - y**2)
(x - y)*(x + y)
```
However, if only part of the expression can participate in the factorization, this fails:
```python
>>> factor(x**2 - y**2 + 1)
x**2 - y**2 + 1
```
So we want a "smarter" factor function, this is a rather common request, I have multiple versions of such a function written by @smichr on the mailing list and in answers on stackoverflow, I'm not up to date on whether any of those are included in sympy itself nowadays, but adapting [one of his functions](https://groups.google.com/g/sympy/c/dxcMAhk8XRM/m/W8kBrjb0IUgJ):
```python
>>> simp = simplify((sin(x)**2 + cos(x)**2) + 2 * (x + y) ** 2)
>>> simp
   2              2    
2⋅x  + 4⋅x⋅y + 2⋅y  + 1
>>> def my_factor(expr):
...     const, nonconst = expr.as_coeff_Add()
...     cand0 = factor(nonconst) + const
...     candidates = {cand0: cand0.count_ops()}
...     for fs in expr.free_symbols:
...         i, d = expr.as_independent(fs, as_Add=True)
...         cand = factor(i) + factor(d)
...         candidates[cand] = cand.count_ops()
...     print(f"{candidates=}")  # debug print, remove in production
...     return min(candidates, key=candidates.__getitem__)
... 

>>> my_factor(simp)
candidates={2*(x + y)**2 + 1: 4, 2*x**2 + 2*y*(2*x + y) + 1: 8, 2*x*(x + 2*y) + 2*y**2 + 1: 8}
         2    
2⋅(x + y)  + 1

```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2530912108/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Adds type annotations to all functions in the quaternion module. Also adds annotations in a few other places that are called by the quaternion module as needed for mypy/pyright.

With these changes the whole module including its test suite can be checked by both mypy and pyright e.g.:
```console
$ mypy sympy/algebras
Success: no issues found in 4 source files
$ pyright sympy/algebras
sympy/sympy/algebras/tests/test_quaternion.py
  sympy/sympy/algebras/tests/test_quaternion.py:168:31 - error: Operator "**" not supported for types "Quaternion" and "float" (reportOperatorIssue)
1 error, 0 warnings, 0 informations 
```
That one complaint from pyright is about a test like
```
    raises(TypeError, lambda: q1**(-0.5))
```
It is not surprising that the type checker finds a type error here. We can add `type: ignore` for these things or more generally it might be better just to disable type checking on the test suite. For now it doesn't matter because mypy accepts it and we don't actually run pyright as a typechecker in CI.

#### Other comments

I think that the quaternion module provides a reasonable demonstration of what some user/downstream code would be like if using only a few core features of sympy. The fact that this code can be fully typed and checks fine under mypy and pyright shows a reasonable milestone in adding annotations. In particular is suggests that this could work for downstream libraries and end users to use a type checker on their code that uses sympy (which has not previously been the case).

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Added documentation for checking whether a number is square free using factorint in the sympy library. This includes an explanation of how to determine if a number is free of prime factors raised to an exponent greater than or equal to k

#### Other comments
This documentation update helps users easily understand how to check if a number is square free using the factorint function.
No changes to the code were made, only improvements to the documentation for clarity.

there,

I'm working with an [algorithm](https://github.com/SymposiumOrganization/NeuralSymbolicRegressionThatScales) that relies on calling `sympy.lambdify` hundreds of millions of times (~200M) and noticed the memory usage of the process steadily creeping up and eventually crashing the program.

I was able to trace the problem to the following (see **Demo** below).

Notably, the memory usage still increases despite
1. lambdifying the same equation again
2. "deleting" the lambdified equation

**System Information:**
WSL2 (Windows 10.0.22631 Build 22631)

```
> lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Release:        22.04
Codename:       jammy
```
```
> python --version
Python 3.11.10
```
```
> pip freeze
mpmath==1.3.0
psutil==6.1.0
sympy==1.13.3
```


### Demo:


```python
# sympy_memory.py
import argparse
import psutil

import sympy


if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Parse command line arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run equation n times')
    parser.add_argument('-n', type=int, default=1, help='Number of repetitions')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Get the current process to monitor memory usage
    process = psutil.Process()

    # Parse an example equation
    equation_str = 'x'
    parsed_equation = sympy.sympify(equation_str)

    print("Memory Usage:")

    for i in range(args.n):
        # Lambdifying the (same) equation in each iteration
        lambdified_equation = sympy.lambdify('x', parsed_equation)

        # Print memory usage every 10000 iterations
        if i % 10000 == 0:
            mem_usage = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024  # Convert to MB
            print(f"{i}/{args.n}: {mem_usage:.2f} MB")

        # "Deleting" the lambdified equation
        del lambdified_equation
```

```
> python sympy_memory.py -n 100000
Memory Usage:
0/100000: 49.91 MB
10000/100000: 54.29 MB
20000/100000: 59.27 MB
30000/100000: 64.17 MB
40000/100000: 68.04 MB
50000/100000: 72.05 MB
60000/100000: 75.91 MB
70000/100000: 79.78 MB
80000/100000: 83.45 MB
90000/100000: 89.08 MB
```

**Observation:** The memory usage increases by about ~~+390KB~~ (correction: 390B https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27216#issuecomment-2453168997) per lambdified equation, totalling +39MB over 100k lambdifications and deletions.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

The C function `log10` is in C89, but in `printing.c` it has been treated as being introduced in C99 instead.  By default this causes an error when attempting to do the printing of `log10` with the standard set to C89.

This adds `log10` to the list of known C89 functions.

#### Other comments

References:
* https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/numeric/math/log10
* K&R

#### Release Notes

  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more

notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

  * Added log10 to the list of C89 functions

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

The C function `log10` is in C89, but in `printing.c` it has been treated as being introduced in C99 instead.  By default this causes an error when attempting to do the printing of `log10` with the standard set to C89.

This adds `log10` to the list of known C89 functions.

#### Other comments

References:
* https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/numeric/math/log10
* K&R

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * Added log10 to the list of C89 functions

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27277","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27277/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27277/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27277/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27277","id":2672104370,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs6fRROy","number":27277,"title":"Adding ways to send stop and continue signals for evaluation functions that can take much time.","user":{"login":"mmatera","id":2862288,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4NjIyODg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2862288?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera","html_url":"https://github.com/mmatera","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mmatera/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-11-19T13:07:51Z","updated_at":"2024-11-19T17:04:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"
In Mathics (https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core), we use Sympy to evaluate expressions.  In some cases, we want to limit the time used for the computation. For that, we put the evaluation in a thread, and if the evaluation takes more than a certain walltime, we abandon the thread and raise a Timeout Exception (see the original code here: https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/blob/master/mathics/eval/sympy.py) 

The problem is that the evaluation continues, and continues consuming resources.  Suppose for example the expression is
```
sympy.integrate(sympy.sin(x)**100000, (x, 0, 1))
```
This reaches any reasonable walltime, and continues computing, until reaches a MaxRecursionError, or a core dump if the maxrecursion is set to a too large number.

It would be useful then to have a way to tell Sympy to stop the evaluation, and then resume it. Below is a simplified example of our code in Mathics ( https://github.com/Mathics3/mathics-core/blob/master/mathics/eval/sympy.py), and where the signals should be sent (`sympy.halt()` and `sympy.resume()`). 


```

from queue import Queue
from threading import Thread

import sympy
from sympy.abc import x


def eval_with_timeout(func, args, walltime=None):
    """

    Parameters
    ----------
    func : Callable
        long evaluation function, like `simplify` or `integrate`.
    args : Tuple
        the arguments of the function.
    walltime : int, optional
        DESCRIPTION. The default is None.

    Raises
    ------
    TimeoutError
        The evaluation was cancel because took too
        long time.

    Returns
    -------
        sympy expression.

    """
    if walltime is None:
        return func(*args)

    def _target(queue):
        args = queue.get()
        try:
            func_result = func(*args)
            queue.put((True, func_result))
        except BaseException as exc:
            queue.put((False, exc))

    queue = Queue(maxsize=1)
    queue.put(args)
    thread = Thread(target=_target, args=(queue,))
    thread.start()
    thread.join(walltime)
    if thread.is_alive():
        # Send the `halt` signal
        ##################
        # sympy.halt()
        ##################
        # Give the chance to allow `func`
        # to acknowledge the `halt` signal
        while thread.is_alive()
            thread.join(0.001)

        # Restore the "working" state
        ##################
        # sympy.resume()
        ###############
        raise TimeoutError

    # pick the result and return
    success, result = queue.get()
    if success:
        return result
    raise result


print("calling with a timeout of 10")
print("  result of type:", type(eval_with_timeout(sympy.integrate,
      (sympy.sin(x)**1000, (x, 0, 1)), walltime=10)))
print("calling with a timeout of 1")
print("  result of type:", type(eval_with_timeout(sympy.integrate,
      (sympy.sin(x)**1000, (x, 0, 1),), walltime=1)))
```


A simple way to implement these signals would be to implement methods that set a global variable `SYMPY_EVALUATION_HALTED`
and use `halt()` and `resume()` to change its state. Then, functions like `integrate` or `simplify` could check the state of this variable to see if the evaluation must be interrupted.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

The argument order for a two-argument `ArcTan[x, y]` in Mathematica differs from the order for `atan2(y, x)` in Sympy, but the printing module prints `atan2(y, x)` as `ArcTan[y, x]` (with the same argument order).

This commit fixes the argument order when printing a two-argument `ArcTan` expression.  This also fixes an existing test that was added in commit 285fd9b233bbc483b006bac409e9c4934a3f1950.

#### Other comments

The parsing module does swap the argument order as expected:

```
>>> from sympy.parsing.mathematica import parse_mathematica
>>> parse_mathematica("ArcTan[x, y]")
atan2(y, x)
```

Other references:
* https://reference.wolfram.com/language/ref/ArcTan.html
* https://functions.wolfram.com/ElementaryFunctions/ArcTan2/

  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

  * Fixed the argument order when printing a two-argument ArcTan Mathematica expression

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In Julia `sinc` is the normalized sinc function, while in SymPy `sinc` is the unnormalized sinc function.  However, the printing module has been printing `sinc(x)` as `sinc(x)` for Julia.

This commit fixes the printing of the `sinc` function by dividing the argument by pi.  The added tests are based on the analogous ones for Octave printing, since Octave's `sinc` is also normalized.

#### Other comments

```
julia> sinc(1)
0
```

Reference:
* https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/math/#Base.Math.sinc

write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is

Fixes the `lambdify` part of gh-27112

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Generated in-line for loops by `PythonCodePrinter` needed to use reverse order of `.args` in `Sum`.

#### Other comments
`.evalf()` on `Sum` with multiple indices is not addressed by this PR.

  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

* utilities
  * `lambdify` now generates correct code for `Sum` instances running over multiple summation indices (nested sums).

This fixes e.g.
```
    In [4]: (x**2 + 10)**2

    100.0⋅⎝0.1⋅x  + 1⎠
```
Extracting a float like this was added in gh-16762 commit c1e25a445f1c27829ea8cf0e04dbdd6d749834ce.

This commit reverts this change because there is no reason to do this and in general it is better to preserve the basic form of the expression if it cannot be substantively simplified by simple rules.

gh-27195
Fixes gh-20927

Reverts gh-16762

This fixes e.g.
```
    In [4]: (x**2 + 10)**2
    Out[4]:
             2
    ⎛ 2     ⎞
    ⎝x  + 10⎠

    In [5]: (x**2 + 10.0)**2
    Out[5]:
                      2
          ⎛     2    ⎞
    100.0⋅⎝0.1⋅x  + 1⎠
```
Extracting a float like this was added in gh-16762 commit c1e25a445f1c27829ea8cf0e04dbdd6d749834ce.

This commit reverts this change because there is no reason to do this and in general it is better to preserve the basic form of the expression if it cannot be substantively simplified by simple rules.

<!-- Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please

open. -->


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed


#### Other comments


#### Release Notes

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* core
   * Floats are no longer extracted from a power of an Add e.g. an expression like `(x**2 + 10.0)**2` will be kept as is. Previously a float was extracted to `100.0*(0.1*x**2 + 1)**2`. This reverts gh-16762 that introduced this change in SymPy 1.5.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27049","id":2513499413,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs6V0PUV","number":27049,"title":"Prefer log2(x) over log(x)/log(2)","user":{"login":"nerai","id":1230531,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMzA1MzE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1230531?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai","html_url":"https://github.com/nerai","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nerai/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-09-09T09:59:11Z","updated_at":"2024-09-09T13:30:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Please pardon my ignorance about SymPy. I'm only a new and indirect user via the PySR library.

In PySR, expressions using log2 are translated to SymPy via `sympy.log(x, 2)`. This is apparently forcibly translated to log(x)/log(2), and shows up in the final result in this form. However, the desired expression just is log2 directly.

To circumvent this, one can instead translate log2 to `sympy.codegen.cfunctions.log2(x)`. This correctly shows up as log2 in the resulting expressions. [Corresponding patch for PySR](https://github.com/MilesCranmer/PySR/pull/712/files)

However, `cfunctions.log2` seems to be an internal, undocumented function. Are we using the right approach? Is there a better documented way to get to the same result?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2338135754","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27049#issuecomment-2338135754","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27049","id":2338135754,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6LXR7K","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-09T13:30:56Z","updated_at":"2024-09-09T13:30:56Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The documentation for log2 is somewhat terse, but it's at least present in the documentation:

Add isnan and isinf support for tensorflow printer

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27112","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27112/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27112/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27112/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27112","id":2548229557,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs6X4uW1","number":27112,"title":"Double sums are not created or evaluated in the correct order in evalf and lambdify","user":{"login":"leonhardpetzel","id":100354556,"node_id":"U_kgDOBftJ_A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/100354556?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel","html_url":"https://github.com/leonhardpetzel","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/leonhardpetzel/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-09-25T14:53:46Z","updated_at":"2024-09-26T06:06:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"To demonstrate, I create a simple double sum:

```
from sympy import lambdify, abc, Sum

expr = Sum(Sum(1, (abc.j, 0, abc.i)), (abc.i, 0, 10))
fun = lambdify((), expr)
print(fun.__doc__)
fun()
```

Output is :
```
...
Expression:
Sum(1, (j, 0, i), (i, 0, 10))
Source code:
def _lambdifygenerated():
    return (builtins.sum(1 for j in range(0, i+1) for i in range(0, 10+1)))
...
```

And the code throws an error, because the python code should be:

```
def _lambdifygenerated():
    return (builtins.sum(1 for i in range(0, 10+1) for j in range(0, i+1)))
```

So, either the translation of two individual Sums into one Sum produces the wrong order. Or the lamdification order needs to be swapped.

Also, I found that evalf and lamdify do not handle double sums in the same way:

```
expr = sympy.Sum(1, (abc.i, 0, 10), (abc.j, 0, abc.i))

print(expr.evalf()) # -> Sum(1, (i, 0, 10), (j, 0, i))
print(lambdify((), expr)()) # -> 66
```
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27112/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27112/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2376014491","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/27112#issuecomment-2376014491","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/27112","id":2376014491,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6Nnxqb","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-26T06:06:13Z","updated_at":"2024-09-26T06:06:13Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Have you tried applying `.doit()` on your expression?
```python
>>> sympy.Sum(1, (abc.i, 0, 10), (abc.j, 0, abc.i)).doit()
11*i + 11

>>> sympy.Sum(1, (abc.j, 0, abc.i), (abc.i, 0, 10)).doit()
66
```
Looks like `sympy.Sum` expects summations to be specified "from the inside out"? 

`lambdify` still does not like this expression though.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2376014491/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```python
>>> Sum(x, (i, 1, j), (j, 1, 5))
  5     j    
 ___   ___   
 ╲     ╲     
  ╲     ╲    
  ╱     ╱   x
 ╱     ╱

As for `.evalf()`, is it reasonable to expect the user to call `.doit()` before `.evalf()`?

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->
Fixes #25173 as far as integer and floating point types are concerned.
The complex type remains broken: it is not native in rust and is typically handled with an external library such as [num-complex](https://crates.io/crates/num-complex)
Matrices, represented as arrays, seem to be fine, as long as the entries are int or float.


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Added automatic type casting in the rust code printer. Integers are always cast to floats if it is detected that an Add, Mul, or Relational expression contains mixed types

The functions floor and ceiling need special treatment, because they return integers in sympy, but floats in rust. Added type-aware versions `float_floor` and `float_ceiling`, specific for rust codegen, that are substituted before printing.

In order for this substitution to work, the automatic rewrites needed to be decoupled from the `_print` method. Now all rewrites happen at once in `rust_code()` before the printing begins. This is a change in behavior: we rewrite the whole expression rather than individual function nodes. I have not stumbled upon any problems with it, but cannot vouch for all use cases.

UPD moved rust_code to from printing.rust to printing.codeprinter, otherwise it ends up polluting the sympy namespace because of new imports
#### Other comments

I'm completely new to the code base, so very open to any suggestions

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:


* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Rust codegen now automatically casts integers to floats in mixed expressions to prevent compilation errors
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Rust codegen now automatically casts integers to floats in mixed expressions to prevent compilation errors
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

It sometimes places floats where only integers are allowed and vice versa.

In rust, `2*arg[1].powi(2)` does not compile because 2 is not a float, 2.0 is. `2.0*arg[1].powi(2.0)` also does not compile, because `powi` expects an integer, not a float.

Currently, sympy does not do this correctly. For example:
![afbeelding](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/assets/56522208/8b5079b6-2d7f-4811-8418-666f61d23489)
is printed as:
```rust
2*m.powi(2)*(φ - φ0)*(4*m.powi(4)*φ.powi(3)*(φ - φ0) + ψ0.powi(2)*(φ.powi(2) - 3)*(φ.powi(2) + 1))(φ*(4*m.powi(4)*φ.powi(2)*(φ - φ0).powi(2) + ψ0.powi(2)*(φ.powi(2) + 1).powi(2))*(2*V0 + m.powi(2)*(φ - φ0).powi(2) - 2*ψ*ψ0))
```

Python 3.11.3
Sympy 1.11.1
Fedora 38, virtual environment ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25173/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25173/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26958","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26958/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26958/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26958/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26958","id":2463897628,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs6S3Bgc","number":26958,"title":"lambdify maps spherical Bessel functions in the wrong way","user":{"login":"AndrejKonforta","id":178315040,"node_id":"U_kgDOCqDfIA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/178315040?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta","html_url":"https://github.com/AndrejKonforta","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/AndrejKonforta/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":200830774,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMDA4MzA3NzQ=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/utilities.lambdify","name":"utilities.lambdify","color":"0000FF","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-08-13T17:49:56Z","updated_at":"2024-08-15T07:36:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Lambdify fails to correctly map the specific partial derivative $\frac{\partial^2 R(n,k,r,r_p)}{\partial r \partial r_p},$ $R(n,k,r,r_p) = j_n(k r_p) (j_n(k r)+j y_n(k r)).$ Here, $j_n$ and $y_n$ are the **spherical** Bessel functions `sympy.functions.special.bessel.jn`, `sympy.functions.special.bessel.yn`. The corresponding numerical routines in scipy are `scipy.special.spherical_jn`, `scipy.special.spherical_yn`, not to be confused with the conventional (cylindrical) Bessel functions `scipy.special.jv`, `scipy.special.yv`. Now, sympy symbolically does resolve the partial derivate in the right way: ```python import sympy as sy n, k, rr, rr_p = sy.symbols("n k r rp") R = sy.functions.special.bessel.jn(n,k*rr_p)*(sy.functions.special.bessel.jn(n,k*rr)+sy.I*sy.functions.special.bessel.yn(n,k*rr)) display(sy.diff(R,rr,rr_p)) ``` $k^{2} \left(j_{n - 1}\left(k rp\right) - \frac{\left(n + 1\right) j_{n}\left(k rp\right)}{k rp}\right) \left(i \left(y_{n - 1}\left(k r\right) - \frac{\left(n + 1\right) y_{n}\left(k r\right)}{k r}\right) + j_{n - 1}\left(k r\right) - \frac{\left(n + 1\right) j_{n}\left(k r\right)}{k r}\right),$ However, if I use lambdify, the wrong result is returned. This can be seen by comparison with the explicit solution manually mapped to scipy: ```python import numpy as np import scipy.special as sp import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # use of sympy's lambdify r_fun_sy = sy.lambdify([n,k,rr,rr_p], sy.diff(R,rr,rr_p), modules=["scipy"], cse=False, docstring_limit=None) # explicit solution via scipy.special.jn, scipy.special.yn def r_fun_sp_a(n,k,rr,rr_p): return k**2*(sp.spherical_jn(n-1,k*rr_p)-(n+1)/k/rr_p*sp.spherical_jn(n,k*rr_p))*\ (1j*(sp.spherical_yn(n-1,k*rr)-(n+1)/k/rr*sp.spherical_yn(n,k*rr)) \ +sp.spherical_jn(n-1,k*rr)-(n+1)/k/rr*sp.spherical_jn(n,k*rr)) # explicit solution via scipy.special.jv, scipy.special.yv def r_fun_sp_b(n,k,rr,rr_p): return k**2*(sp.jv(n-1,k*rr_p)-(n+1)/k/rr_p*sp.jv(n,k*rr_p))*\ (1j*(sp.yv(n-1,k*rr)-(n+1)/k/rr*sp.yv(n,k*rr)) \ +sp.jv(n-1,k*rr)-(n+1)/k/rr*sp.jv(n,k*rr)) # e.g. kk = 1 nn = 1 x_0 = 1 r_v = np.linspace(1,20,50) R_t1 = [r_fun_sy(nn,kk,_,x_0) for _ in r_v] R_t2_a = [r_fun_sp_a(nn,kk,_,x_0) for _ in r_v] R_t2_b = [r_fun_sp_b(nn,kk,_,x_0) for _ in r_v] fig = plt.figure() ax = fig.add_subplot(1,1,1) ax.plot(r_v, np.real(R_t1), label="sympy", color='tab:red') ax.plot(r_v, np.real(R_t2_a), label="scipy, jn, yn", color='tab:blue') ax.plot(r_v, np.real(R_t2_b), label="scipy, jv, yv", color='tab:green', linestyle='dashed') ax.grid() ax.legend() ``` The plot shows that the solution via lambdify does not agree with the result obtained by use of scipy's spherial Bessel routines. However, if `scipy.special.spherical_jn`, `scipy.special.spherical_yn` are replaced by `scipy.special.jv`, `scipy.special.yv` (cylindrical Bessel) respectively, we get the lambdify solution. This is however wrong: According to the documentation, `sympy.functions.special.bessel.jn`, `sympy.functions.special.bessel.yn` correspond to the **spherical** Bessel functions. An inspection of the lambdified expression confirms that indeed `sympy.functions.special.bessel.jn` is mapped to `jn` which eventually is mapped to `scipy.special.jv` instead of `scipy.special.spherical_jn` ```python import inspect, numpy lines = inspect.getsource(r_fun) print(lines) print(r_fun.__globals__['jn']) numpy.info(r_fun.__globals__['jn']) ``` ``` def _lambdifygenerated(n, k, r, rp): return k**2*(jn(n - 1, k*rp) - (n + 1)*jn(n, k*rp)/(k*rp))*(1j*(yn(n - 1, k*r) - (n + 1)*yn(n, k*r)/(k*r)) + jn(n - 1, k*r) - (n + 1)*jn(n, k*r)/(k*r)) ### numpy.info returns the docstring of scipy.special.jv ! jv(x1, x2, /, out=None, *, where=True, casting='same_kind', order='K', dtype=None, subok=True[, signature, extobj]) jv(v, z, out=None) Bessel function of the first kind of real order and complex argument. Parameters ---------- v : array_like Order (float). z : array_like Argument (float or complex). out : ndarray, optional Optional output array for the function values ``` Perhaps this bug is due to the confusing naming convention, but as of now, apparently wrong results are returned. expression","user":{"login":"zexuan-zhou","id":23016825,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMDE2ODI1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23016825?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou","html_url":"https://github.com/zexuan-zhou","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zexuan-zhou/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":3014667366,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMDE0NjY3MzY2","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/core.relational","name":"core.relational","color":"FB7CA2","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":16,"created_at":"2024-07-09T18:34:37Z","updated_at":"2024-07-11T06:41:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, I'm new to Sympy and I wonder how to cast null value to a default value in a Sympy expression
For example
```
import sympy as sp
x = sp.symbols("x")
# Return 0 if x is of nan value, and 1 otherwise
exp = sp.Piecewise((0.0, sp.Eq(x, sp.nan)), (1.0, True))
sp.lambdify((x), exp)(x=float('nan'))
Out[28]: 1.0
```
The above expression returns 1.0 instead of 0.0
I found that the problem comes from the expression
```
In [29]: sp.Eq(x, sp.nan)
Out[29]: False
```
It seems that `sp.Eq` does symbolic evaluation on variable `x` instead of waiting for the input from the actual calculation

Maybe I'm using sympy in a wrong way. But would appreciate someone to shed some light on how to do this

```python
>>> omo = Add(oo, -oo, evaluate=False)
>>> omo
-∞ + ∞
>>> omo.simplify()
nan
>>> omo.is_number
True
>>> isnan(omo)  # with a call to simplify in isnan.eval, this would return the expected "true"
False
```
in general `.simplify` is too expensive to call. Do you think it is safe to assume that is always cheap to evaluate simplify when `.is_number` is True?

It is quite common throughout SymPy that subsequent operations on any expression built using `evaluate=False` do not work as expected, so I'm not too worried about this edge case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2223936940/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'm new to Sympy and I wonder how to cast null value to a default value in a Sympy expression
For example
```
import sympy as sp
x = sp.symbols("x")
# Return 0 if x is of nan value, and 1 otherwise
exp = sp.Piecewise((0.0, sp.Eq(x, sp.nan)), (1.0, True))
sp.lambdify((x), exp)(x=float('nan'))
Out[28]: 1.0
```
The above expression returns 1.0 instead of 0.0
I found that the problem comes from the expression
```
In [29]: sp.Eq(x, sp.nan)
Out[29]: False
```
It seems that `sp.Eq` does symbolic evaluation on variable `x` instead of waiting for the input from the actual calculation

Maybe I'm using sympy in a wrong way. But would appreciate someone to shed some light on how to do this

Thank you","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26784/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26784/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2237497448","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26784#issuecomment-2237497448","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26784","id":2237497448,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6FXYBo","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-18T20:24:06Z","updated_at":"2024-07-18T20:24:06Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Yes, that's expected. I'm new to Sympy and I wonder how to cast null value to a default value in a Sympy expression
For example
```
import sympy as sp
x = sp.symbols("x")
# Return 0 if x is of nan value, and 1 otherwise
exp = sp.Piecewise((0.0, sp.Eq(x, sp.nan)), (1.0, True))
sp.lambdify((x), exp)(x=float('nan'))
Out[28]: 1.0
```
The above expression returns 1.0 instead of 0.0
I found that the problem comes from the expression
```
In [29]: sp.Eq(x, sp.nan)
Out[29]: False
```
It seems that `sp.Eq` does symbolic evaluation on variable `x` instead of waiting for the input from the actual calculation

Maybe I'm using sympy in a wrong way. But would appreciate someone to shed some light on how to do this

Thank you","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26784/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26784/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2240051100","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26784#issuecomment-2240051100","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26784","id":2240051100,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6FhHec","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-19T20:20:10Z","updated_at":"2024-07-19T20:20:10Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Typically we do not self-merge PRs in the sympy project. The [goal](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/25913#issue-2003988596) of this error message was of course to waste *less* research time (since sympy used to silently generate broken code), but we (I) might have unintentionally introduced a bug, so thanks for opening an issue (I think we all want the same thing here, and everyone involved are donating their time).

From your repository I could create this reproducer:
```python
import mpmath
from sympy import Symbol, zeta, lambdify, Derivative

s = Symbol('s')
z = zeta(s)
zp = Derivative(z, s)
f = lambdify(s, zp, modules=['mpmath', {'Derivative': lambda expr, z: mpmath.zeta(z, derivative=1)}])
print(f(2))
```
which fails for the merge commit (b93a982) of gh-25913 (but passes when doing `git checkout b93a982~1`).

If you print the docstring of our generated function `f` for the older version of sympy it looks like this:
```
>>> print(f.__doc__)
Created with lambdify. Signature:

func(s)

Expression:

Derivative(zeta(s), s)

Source code:

def _lambdifygenerated(s):
    return (  # Not supported in Python with mpmath:
  # Derivative
Derivative(zeta(s), s))


Imported modules:

```

I see that there's a printer setting called "allow_unknown_functions" (which [looks like it is being enabled](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/2565eb35e255f0fee2267f20e9065b0f5eedf562/sympy/utilities/lambdify.py#L833) by lambdify) that alters when `CodePrinter` calls the method `_print_not_supported`:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/2565eb35e255f0fee2267f20e9065b0f5eedf562/sympy/printing/codeprinter.py#L458

So `.is_Function` needs to be True, let's check if that holds for our `Derivative` instance:
```python
>>> zp.is_Function
False
```
So, with a bit of luck it would be enough to change that if-statement to `if (expr.is_Function or expr.is_Derivative) ...`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2146792774/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
>>> f = lambdify(s, zp, modules=['mpmath', {'Derivative': lambda expr, z: mpmath.zeta(z, derivative=1)}])
```
*any* Derivative instance would map to mpmath.zeta, an expression containing different derivatives would return non-meaningful results.

The proper fix would be to first replace the particular derivative of interest with a `Function` instance (e.g. `Function('my_zeta_derivative')(s)`. And then pass this function as a known function to `lambdify`. That this ever "worked" is mostly an accident.

Furthermore, the previous generated code performed unnecessary work (it evaluated the primitive function at the point of interest). I have a local patch of `codeprinter.py` which generates a "functioning" lambdify callable:
```
(Pdb) print(f.__doc__)
Created with lambdify. Signature:

func(s)

Expression:

Derivative(zeta(s), s)

Source code:

def _lambdifygenerated(s):
    return Derivative(zeta(s), (s, 1))


Imported modules:

```

but this is rather pointless. If we can come up with a legitimate use of passing `'Derivative'` as a known function, I can use that as a test case for my patch, and submit it as a PR. But the original code snippet to reproduce the issue is not suitable since it doesn't do what you might expect.

The proper way to customize how derivatives are evaluated in the lambdified function would probably be along the lines of:
```python
>>> from sympy import symbols, zeta, lambdify, Function
>>> import mpmath
>>> s = symbols('s')
>>> expr = 42 + 17*zeta(s).diff(s)
>>> zeta_diff = Function('zeta_diff')(s)
>>> expr2 = expr.replace(zeta(s).diff(s), zeta_diff)
>>> expr2
17*zeta_diff(s) + 42
>>> f = lambdify(s, expr2, ['mpmath', {'zeta_diff': lambda arg: mpmath.zeta(arg, derivative=1)}])
>>> f(2)
mpf('26.061679676630654')
```

was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
- gh-26663
- https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11802#issuecomment-602855301

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Currently `lambdify` cannot properly handle `Derivative` instances. This PR allows printers to add methods for printing `Derivative` instances, which then allows lambdify to work with those seamlessly. If the special printing method `_print_Derivative_####` is not present, the exception thrown will indicate this in its error message.

#### Other comments
If this approach is approved, then I will:

- [ ] Describe `_print_Derivative_####` in the documentation for our printer classes.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->
