When I try to dig deeper here I do wonder if the previous generated functions actually worked? I tried to call `f_grad` in your example with some real valued arguments but using earlier released versions of sympy, I get `NameError` when calling those:
<details>
<summary>slightly modifed version of ericpre's reproducer</summary>

```python
import sympy

def main(expr_str):
    expr = sympy.sympify(expr_str)

    x = [symbol for symbol in expr.free_symbols if symbol.name == "x"][0]
    variables = sympy.symbols([s.name for s in expr.free_symbols])
    real_variables = sympy.symbols([s.name for s in variables], real=True)

    # Replace symbols by real symbols to help with differentiation
    # according to git comment 9 years ago
    expr = expr.subs(
        {orig: real_ for (orig, real_) in zip(variables, real_variables)}
    )

    parameters = [var for var in real_variables if var.name != "x"]
    eval_expr = expr.evalf()

    print(f"{expr_str=} {real_variables}")
    print('='*40)
    print("")
    for p in parameters:
        grad_expr = sympy.diff(eval_expr, p)
        gef = grad_expr.evalf()
        f_grad = sympy.utilities.lambdify(
            real_variables,
            gef,
            modules="numpy",
            dummify=False,
        )
        print(f"{p=}\n  {grad_expr=}\n  {gef=}")
        n = len(real_variables)
        res = f_grad(*[i/(n+1) for i in range(n)])

if __name__ == '__main__':
    for expr_str in ["A*heaviside(x-n,0.5)", "A * arctan(k * (x - x0))"]:
        main(expr_str)
        print("\n"*3)
```

</details>

```py
from sympy import *
from sympy.vector import CoordSys3D, gradient

t = symbols("t")
N = CoordSys3D("N")
i, j, k = N.base_vectors()
xn, yn, zn = N.base_scalars()
expr = -(xn**2) * tan(t) ** 2 + yn**2 + zn**2
g = gradient(expr)
m = g.magnitude()
vec = g / m
components = vec.to_matrix(N)
for i in range(3):
    print(f"component {i}:", components[i])

# component 0: -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 1: 2*N.y/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 2: 2*N.z/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.12.1. Note that instances of `BaseScalar` are converted to dummy symbols.

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

```text
Help on function _lambdifygenerated:

_lambdifygenerated(Dummy_41, Dummy_40, Dummy_39, t)
    Created with lambdify. Signature:

    func(N.x, N.y, N.z, t)

    Expression:

    -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)

    Source code:

    def _lambdifygenerated(Dummy_41, Dummy_40, Dummy_39, t):
        return -2*Dummy_41*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*Dummy_39**2 + 4*Dummy_40**2 + 4*Dummy_41**2*tan(t)**4)


    Imported modules:

None
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.13 rc1: no longer supported. That was essential to properly plot vector fields [with my plotting module](https://github.com/Davide-sd/sympy-plot-backends/). 

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

```python
>>> srepr(sympify('arctan(x)'))
"Function('arctan')(Symbol('x'))"

>>> srepr(sympify('atan(x)'))
"atan(Symbol('x'))"
```

`parse_expr` can offer more customization:
```python
>>> parse_expr('atan(x)', transformations=(), local_dict={'x': x})
atan(x)
>>> parse_expr('atan(x)', transformations=(), local_dict={'x': x})
...
NameError: name 'arctan' is not defined
```

code:

```py
from sympy import *
from sympy.vector import CoordSys3D, gradient

t = symbols("t")
N = CoordSys3D("N")
i, j, k = N.base_vectors()
xn, yn, zn = N.base_scalars()
expr = -(xn**2) * tan(t) ** 2 + yn**2 + zn**2
g = gradient(expr)
m = g.magnitude()
vec = g / m
components = vec.to_matrix(N)
for i in range(3):
    print(f"component {i}:", components[i])

# component 0: -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 1: 2*N.y/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 2: 2*N.z/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.12.1. Note that instances of `BaseScalar` are converted to dummy symbols.

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

```text
Help on function _lambdifygenerated:

_lambdifygenerated(Dummy_41, Dummy_40, Dummy_39, t)
    Created with lambdify. Signature:

    func(N.x, N.y, N.z, t)

    Expression:

    -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)

    Source code:

    def _lambdifygenerated(Dummy_41, Dummy_40, Dummy_39, t):
        return -2*Dummy_41*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*Dummy_39**2 + 4*Dummy_40**2 + 4*Dummy_41**2*tan(t)**4)


    Imported modules:

None
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.13 rc1: no longer supported. That was essential to properly plot vector fields [with my plotting module](https://github.com/Davide-sd/sympy-plot-backends/). 

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

Is that a well formed input? Should we throw when the inner arguments are `Expr` instead of `Symbol`?

Currently `.diff()` gives an expression with `Subs`:
```python
>>> zeta(x**2).diff(x)
2*x*Subs(Derivative(zeta(_xi_1), _xi_1), _xi_1, x**2)
```
and now I discover that this isn't handled by `lambdify`, it would perhaps be useful if we could make that work here as well:
```python
>>> f = lambdift(x, zeta(x**2).diff(x), modules=['mpmath'])
>>> f(2)
...
NameError: name 'Subs' is not defined

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
- gh-26663
- https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11802#issuecomment-602855301

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Currently `lambdify` cannot properly handle `Derivative` instances. This PR allows printers to add methods for printing `Derivative` instances, which then allows lambdify to work with those seamlessly. If the special printing method `_print_Derivative_####` is not present, the exception thrown will indicate this in its error message.

#### Other comments
If this approach is approved, then I will:

- [ ] Describe `_print_Derivative_####` in the documentation for our printer classes.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
- gh-26663
- https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11802#issuecomment-602855301

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Currently `lambdify` cannot properly handle `Derivative` instances. This PR allows printers to add methods for printing `Derivative` instances, which then allows lambdify to work with those seamlessly. If the special printing method `_print_Derivative_####` is not present, the exception thrown will indicate this in its error message.

#### Other comments
If this approach is approved, then I will:

- [ ] Describe `_print_Derivative_####` in the documentation for our printer classes.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

code:

```py
from sympy import *
from sympy.vector import CoordSys3D, gradient

t = symbols("t")
N = CoordSys3D("N")
i, j, k = N.base_vectors()
xn, yn, zn = N.base_scalars()
expr = -(xn**2) * tan(t) ** 2 + yn**2 + zn**2
g = gradient(expr)
m = g.magnitude()
vec = g / m
components = vec.to_matrix(N)
for i in range(3):
    print(f"component {i}:", components[i])

# component 0: -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 1: 2*N.y/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 2: 2*N.z/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.12.1. Note that instances of `BaseScalar` are converted to dummy symbols.

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

```text
Help on function _lambdifygenerated:

_lambdifygenerated(Dummy_41, Dummy_40, Dummy_39, t)
    Created with lambdify. Signature:

    func(N.x, N.y, N.z, t)

    Expression:

    -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)

    Source code:

    def _lambdifygenerated(Dummy_41, Dummy_40, Dummy_39, t):
        return -2*Dummy_41*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*Dummy_39**2 + 4*Dummy_40**2 + 4*Dummy_41**2*tan(t)**4)


    Imported modules:

None
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.13 rc1: no longer supported. That was essential to properly plot vector fields [with my plotting module](https://github.com/Davide-sd/sympy-plot-backends/). 

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

Yes, please open new issues if you find a failure mode that was present even before `PrintMethodNotImplementedError`. As for `DiracDelta` I remember that its support in lambdify has been discussed (you can search for DiracDelta and lambdify, I couldn't immediately find the comment I was thinking of) and it was decided that we should not generate code for it by default.

The rationale is rather simple: people generally want useful (floating point) results when using lambdify, having e.g. a python code printer emit something like `(float('inf') if x==0 else 0.0)` is generally not helpful, and it can be quite hard to debug later in the process if expressions are big ("why is my nonlinear solver not converging?").

If this really is something that a user wants, then they can pass this explicitly as a user-known function to `lambdify`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2160451385/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

code:

```py
from sympy import *
from sympy.vector import CoordSys3D, gradient

t = symbols("t")
N = CoordSys3D("N")
i, j, k = N.base_vectors()
xn, yn, zn = N.base_scalars()
expr = -(xn**2) * tan(t) ** 2 + yn**2 + zn**2
g = gradient(expr)
m = g.magnitude()
vec = g / m
components = vec.to_matrix(N)
for i in range(3):
    print(f"component {i}:", components[i])

# component 0: -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 1: 2*N.y/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
# component 2: 2*N.z/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.12.1. Note that instances of `BaseScalar` are converted to dummy symbols.

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

```text
Help on function _lambdifygenerated:

    Created with lambdify. Signature:

    func(N.x, N.y, N.z, t)

    Expression:

    -2*N.x*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*N.x**2*tan(t)**4 + 4*N.y**2 + 4*N.z**2)

    Source code:

    def _lambdifygenerated(Dummy_41, Dummy_40, Dummy_39, t):
        return -2*Dummy_41*tan(t)**2/sqrt(4*Dummy_39**2 + 4*Dummy_40**2 + 4*Dummy_41**2*tan(t)**4)


    Imported modules:

None
```

Lambdifying with sympy 1.13 rc1: no longer supported. That was essential to properly plot vector fields [with my plotting module](https://github.com/Davide-sd/sympy-plot-backends/). 

```py
f = lambdify([xn, yn, zn, t], components[0])
print(help(f))
```

translation of lerchphi into Mathematica","user":{"login":"rogerbalsach","id":59893197,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU5ODkzMTk3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/59893197?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach","html_url":"https://github.com/rogerbalsach","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rogerbalsach/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":84627347,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4NDYyNzM0Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/printing","name":"printing","color":"0000FF","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-06-14T18:08:15Z","updated_at":"2024-06-14T18:21:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The `lerchphi` function is currently translated to Mathematica as follows:
```python
from sympy import lerchphi, mathematica_code
from sympy.abc import z, s, a

expr = lerchphi(z, s, a)
print(mathematica_code(expr))  # Out: LerchPhi[z, s, a]
```

However in Mathematica there are two different functions: [LerchPhi](https://reference.wolfram.com/language/ref/LerchPhi.html) and [HurwitzLerchPhi](https://reference.wolfram.com/language/ref/HurwitzLerchPhi.html).
Sympy's implementation of `lerchphi` corresponds to the second one. This can be seen by evaluating `lerchphi(0.5, I, I - 1)`:
```python
from sympy import lerchphi

print(lerchphi(0.5, 1j, 1j-1).evalf())  # Out: 13.0159323472912 - 4.06716554582987*I
```

Which should be compared with
[LerchPhi](https://www.wolframalpha.com/input?i=LerchPhi%5B0.5%2C+I%2C+I+-+1%5D): `LerchPhi[0.5, I, I-1] = 3.52136 -0.638198 i`
[HurwitzLerchPhi](https://www.wolframalpha.com/input?i=HurwitzLerchPhi%5B0.5%2C+I%2C+I+-+1%5D): `HurwitzLerchPhi[0.5, I, I-1] = 13.0159 -4.06717 i`","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26707/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26707/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2168543355","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26707#issuecomment-2168543355","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26707","id":2168543355,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs6BQVh7","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-14T18:21:31Z","updated_at":"2024-06-14T18:21:31Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thank you @rogerbalsach for reporting this, I don't know how many of the current developers have access to mathematica, so any help in fixing that printer is most welcome. It looks like printing of lerchphi was added in this commit:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/commit/b231d6ab4aeab4049b23d48782e8bd9b580b69a5

Maybe there are even other errors hiding?

Anyhow, a pull request by someone with access to mathematica (who can verify correctness) would be most welcome.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2168543355/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
- gh-26663
- https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11802#issuecomment-602855301

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Currently `lambdify` cannot properly handle `Derivative` instances. This PR allows printers to add methods for printing `Derivative` instances, which then allows lambdify to work with those seamlessly. If the special printing method `_print_Derivative_####` is not present, the exception thrown will indicate this in its error message.

#### Other comments
If this approach is approved, then I will:

- [ ] Describe `_print_Derivative_####` in the documentation for our printer classes.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
- gh-26663
- https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11802#issuecomment-602855301

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Currently `lambdify` cannot properly handle `Derivative` instances. This PR allows printers to add methods for printing `Derivative` instances, which then allows lambdify to work with those seamlessly. If the special printing method `_print_Derivative_####` is not present, the exception thrown will indicate this in its error message.

#### Other comments
If this approach is approved, then I will:

- [ ] Describe `_print_Derivative_####` in the documentation for our printer classes.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
- gh-26663
- https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11802#issuecomment-602855301

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Currently `lambdify` cannot properly handle `Derivative` instances. This PR allows printers to add methods for printing `Derivative` instances, which then allows lambdify to work with those seamlessly. If the special printing method `_print_Derivative_####` is not present, the exception thrown will indicate this in its error message.

#### Other comments
If this approach is approved, then I will:

- [ ] Describe `_print_Derivative_####` in the documentation for our printer classes.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

Your title above should be a short description of what
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
- gh-26663
- https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11802#issuecomment-602855301

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Currently `lambdify` cannot properly handle `Derivative` instances. This PR allows printers to add methods for printing `Derivative` instances, which then allows lambdify to work with those seamlessly. If the special printing method `_print_Derivative_####` is not present, the exception thrown will indicate this in its error message.

#### Other comments
If this approach is approved, then I will:

- [ ] Describe `_print_Derivative_####` in the documentation for our printer classes.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * Subclasses of CodePrinter can now customize printing of `Derivative` instances
    by implementing e.g. `_print_Derivative_zeta(args, deriv_orders)`. This allows lambdify
    to work with expression involving unexpanded `Derivative` instances.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### References to other Issues or PRs

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
* Enables ruff rule015 and which tries to avoid a temporary data struct just to access the first element of an iterable. Instead, it will try use the `next(iter(x))` idiom to access the element. Not only does the prevent the temporary list or tuple for taking up a massive amount of memory, it turns an `O(n)` operation into an `O(1)` one.

#### Other comments

Notes and instructions for merging this PR: 1. Please merge the PR only after the tests have passed. 2. Feel free to push to the bot's branch to update this PR if needed. **Please note that if you close this PR we presume that the feedstock has been rebuilt, so if you are going to perform the rebuild yourself don't close this PR until the your rebuild has been merged.**
Here are some more details about this specific migrator: > > TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot > PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details). > The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release > channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main > `conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA. > > The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy > version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy > will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions. > > Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a > run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a > run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove > any occurrences of this. > > However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package > is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should > check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you > need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy > issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages). > > Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the > final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages > that can be published to our main channels. > > If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do > ``` > conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc > ``` > or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly. > > ### To-Dos: > * [x] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility) > * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`. > * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases. > * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`. > * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed. > > PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that > the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until > a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this > feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in > case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).

{"repository_id":135185894,"push_id":18496848763,"size":3,"distinct_size":3,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"cb4136cd2285bbc628fdf4c5fade3fc6ea32ad7e","before":"bf9b75580ec044e83c0ae41a7eb813781dc6357d","commits":[{"sha":"9f9206f4a03a50fe06da0deea2ddc4589ba9e0db","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"Rebuild for numpy 2.0

TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot
PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details).
The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release
channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main
`conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA.

The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy
version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy
will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions.

Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a
run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a
run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove
any occurrences of this.

However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package
is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should
check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you
need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy
issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages).

Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the
final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages
that can be published to our main channels.

If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do
```
conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc
```
or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly.

### To-Dos:
  * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility)
    * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`.
    * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases.
    * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`.
  * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed.

PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that
the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until
a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this
feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in

{"repository_id":135185902,"push_id":18496850993,"size":3,"distinct_size":3,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"46fb4aa5f023c6f295f041cc4a6886fc3f51bd16","before":"64c1acd33585e9440a0af6f25b2bb94f0c784534","commits":[{"sha":"695174fb78495a56687392a05c25acecb9f500e4","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"Rebuild for numpy 2.0

TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot
PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details).
The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release
channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main
`conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA.

The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy
version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy
will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions.

Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a
run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a
run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove
any occurrences of this.

However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package
is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should
check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you
need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy
issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages).

Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the
final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages
that can be published to our main channels.

If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do
```
conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc
```
or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly.

### To-Dos:
  * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility)
    * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`.
    * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases.
    * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`.
  * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed.

PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that
the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until
a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this
feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in
case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pygslodeiv2-feedstock/commits/695174fb78495a56687392a05c25acecb9f500e4"},{"sha":"e75ca1664722a29de6ada9352b32f072c1b33a95","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"MNT: Re-rendered with conda-build 24.5.0, conda-smithy 3.35.1, and conda-forge-pinning 2024.05.18.00.22.24","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pygslodeiv2-feedstock/commits/e75ca1664722a29de6ada9352b32f072c1b33a95"},{"sha":"46fb4aa5f023c6f295f041cc4a6886fc3f51bd16","author":{"email":"e3d919cee7b807dd94ceca90b782803464073bc6@gmail.com","name":"Bjorn"},"message":"Merge pull request #23 from regro-cf-autotick-bot/rebuild-numpy2-0-1_h5fc115

{"repository_id":135185908,"push_id":18500244717,"size":5,"distinct_size":5,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"4fbf0c8321951fd8a1ae2737df01e4c8d8deaa82","before":"c1e9fe3235f16632500a04cd7ae933bdd56f09c4","commits":[{"sha":"6531ca58a3c4f438f87d237b785e40dbda89fa17","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"Rebuild for numpy 2.0

TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot
PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details).
The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release
channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main
`conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA.

The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy
version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy
will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions.

Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a
run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a
run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove
any occurrences of this.

However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package
is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should
check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you
need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy
issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages).

Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the
final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages
that can be published to our main channels.

If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do
```
conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc
```
or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly.

### To-Dos:
  * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility)
    * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`.
    * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases.
    * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`.
  * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed.

PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that
the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until
a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this
feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in
case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pyodeint-feedstock/commits/6531ca58a3c4f438f87d237b785e40dbda89fa17"},{"sha":"1bbe2d0b8f41faeb9071832f9b246679d6fa0847","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"MNT: Re-rendered with conda-build 24.5.0, conda-smithy 3.35.1, and conda-forge-pinning 2024.05.18.00.22.24","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pyodeint-feedstock/commits/1bbe2d0b8f41faeb9071832f9b246679d6fa0847"},{"sha":"a66417fa56e1d47e9dbffc9acd7f513531faa166","author":{"email":"e3d919cee7b807dd94ceca90b782803464073bc6@gmail.com","name":"Björn Ingvar Dahlgren"},"message":"add setuptools","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pyodeint-feedstock/commits/a66417fa56e1d47e9dbffc9acd7f513531faa166"},{"sha":"8830fb7359c07d6254bf102adbe287cbefa9e76d","author":{"email":"e3d919cee7b807dd94ceca90b782803464073bc6@gmail.com","name":"Björn Ingvar Dahlgren"},"message":"bump release to 0.10.7","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pyodeint-feedstock/commits/8830fb7359c07d6254bf102adbe287cbefa9e76d"},{"sha":"4fbf0c8321951fd8a1ae2737df01e4c8d8deaa82","author":{"email":"e3d919cee7b807dd94ceca90b782803464073bc6@gmail.com","name":"Bjorn"},"message":"Merge pull request #28 from regro-cf-autotick-bot/rebuild-numpy2-0-1_haba389

{"repository_id":135658743,"push_id":18554514820,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"e8f9d8ed29623eb9851801c7a8fa55e26f7f7485","before":"0c72ec326acdfc45b3381953ba613abaed73bf4f","commits":[{"sha":"e8f9d8ed29623eb9851801c7a8fa55e26f7f7485","author":{"email":"75d0c4ad517d85c3745aa112e45f2e479cb18cc2@users.noreply.github.com","name":"regro-cf-autotick-bot"},"message":"Rebuild for numpy 2.0 (#15)

* Rebuild for numpy 2.0

TL;DR: The way we build against numpy has changed as of numpy 2.0. This bot
PR has updated the recipe to account for the changes (see below for details).
The numpy 2.0 package itself is currently only available from a special release
channel (`conda-forge/label/numpy_rc`) and will not be available on the main
`conda-forge` channel until the release of numpy 2.0 GA.

The biggest change is that we no longer need to use the oldest available numpy
version at build time in order to support old numpy version at runtime - numpy
will by default use a compatible ABI for the oldest still-supported numpy versions.

Additionally, we no longer need to use `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` as a
run requirement - this has been handled for more than two years now by a
run-export on the numpy package itself. The migrator will therefore remove
any occurrences of this.

However, by default, building against numpy 2.0 will assume that the package
is compatible with numpy 2.0, which is not necessarily the case. You should
check that the upstream package explicitly supports numpy 2.0, otherwise you
need to add a `- numpy <2` run requirement until that happens (check numpy
issue 26191 for an overview of the most important packages).

Note that the numpy release candidate promises to be ABI-compatible with the
final 2.0 release. This means that building against 2.0.0rc1 produces packages
that can be published to our main channels.

If you already want to use the numpy 2.0 release candidate yourself, you can do
```
conda config --add channels conda-forge/label/numpy_rc
```
or add this channel to your `.condarc` file directly.

### To-Dos:
  * [ ] Match run-requirements for numpy (i.e. check upstream `pyproject.toml` or however the project specifies numpy compatibility)
    * If upstream is not yet compatible with numpy 2.0, add `numpy <2` upper bound under `run:`.
    * If upstream is already compatible with numpy 2.0, nothing else should be necessary in most cases.
    * If upstream requires a minimum numpy version newer than 1.19, you can add `numpy >=x.y` under `run:`.
  * [ ] Remove any remaining occurrences of `{{ pin_compatible("numpy") }}` that the bot may have missed.

PS. If the build does not compile anymore, this is almost certainly a sign that
the upstream project is not yet ready for numpy 2.0; do not close this PR until
a version compatible with numpy 2.0 has been released upstream and on this
feedstock (in the meantime, you can keep the bot from reopening this PR in
case of git conflicts by marking it as a draft).

* MNT: Re-rendered with conda-build 24.5.0, conda-smithy 3.36.0, and conda-forge-pinning 2024.05.21.18.33.22

* explicitly add cython to enable recythonization of .pyx

* skip benign test

* try to escape quotes

* double quotes?

* try to escape space

* use patch instead

* relax the non-strict test further

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26522/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26522/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2142890404","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/26522#issuecomment-2142890404","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26522","id":2142890404,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs5_uemk","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-31T19:50:20Z","updated_at":"2024-05-31T19:50:20Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I'm not personally a fan of having a flag which changes the behavior of methods of class, I much rather have two distinctly named classes (their relation, i.e. one being the child of the other, or them both sharing a common parent class is to be considered an implementation detail in my opinion).

Math constants are spelled differently for one. I see `inf` in this PR, Java's `Double.NaN` also comes to mind. Java also supports both single and double precision floats, JavaScript only the latter.

But some math constants are spelled the same way, e.g. `Math.pi`.

Integer division differs between the two, since JavaScript doesn't really have a first class integer type.

If we want to support special functions, I guess we could default to Apache commons math library for Java. But that can of course wait to a later date.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/2142890404/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26453","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26453/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26453/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26453/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26453","id":2223132819,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs6EglCT","number":26453,"title":"`simplify` hangs for complex, trigonometric, surd expression ","user":{"login":"TysonRayJones","id":29574749,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI5NTc0NzQ5","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/29574749?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones","html_url":"https://github.com/TysonRayJones","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TysonRayJones/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-04-03T14:42:55Z","updated_at":"2024-04-04T16:01:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi there, Thanks very much for your work on a brilliant library! I'm using Sympy version `1.12` in Python `3.11.7 `, and trying to understand why calling `simplify()` on the below expression (where `w,x,y` are general, i.e. complex) hangs: ``` expr = sqrt(2)*(-2*w - 1)*sin(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2)/2 + cos(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2)/2)*cos(y/2 + conjugate(y)/2) - (sqrt(2)*(w**2 + 1)*cos(x/2 - conjugate(x)/2)/2 + I*sin(x/2 - conjugate(x)/2)/2)*sin(y/2 + conjugate(y)/2 expr.simplify() ``` To help diagnose, here is the successful evaluation of `simplify()` upon all the constituents of `expr` above. Only the final expression (the difference of `c` and `d` below) invokes a hang: ```python from sympy import symbols, sqrt, sin, cos, conjugate, I # complex variables w, x, y = symbols('w x y') # Re[x], Re[y] xAlt = x/2 + conjugate(x)/2 yAlt = y/2 + conjugate(y)/2 # ~ cos(Re[y]) ( cos(Re[y]) - (...) sin(Re[x]) ) a = sqrt(2)*(-2*w - 1)*sin(xAlt)/2 + cos(xAlt)/2 # ~ sin(Re[y]) ( (...) cosh(Im[y]) - sinh(Im[x]) ) b = sqrt(2)*(w**2 + 1)*cos(xAlt)/2 + I*sin(xAlt)/2 c = a * cos(yAlt) d = b * sin(yAlt) expr = c - d # evals fine, immediately print('a:', a.simplify()) print('b:', b.simplify()) print('c:', c.simplify()) print('d:', d.simplify()) # hangs print('expr:', expr.simplify()) ``` The four preceding simplifys do nothing (which is fine), so output: ``` a: -sqrt(2)*(2*w + 1)*sin(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2)/2 + cos(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2)/2 b: sqrt(2)*(w**2 + 1)*cos(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2)/2 + I*sin(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2)/2 c: -(sqrt(2)*(2*w + 1)*sin(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2) - cos(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2))*cos(y/2 + conjugate(y)/2)/2 d: (sqrt(2)*(w**2 + 1)*cos(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2) + I*sin(x/2 + conjugate(x)/2))*sin(y/2 + conjugate(y)/2)/2 ``` Mathematica's [`FullSimplify`](https://reference.wolfram.com/language/ref/FullSimplify.html) does not attempt to simplify this expression further than simply substituting `x/2 + conjugate(x/2)` for `re(x)` (similarly for `y`): Note that I do __not__ actually need this simplified expression. The _unsimplified_ form of `expr` above is actually fine in my application. I do however need to _attempt_ to simplify this (and an extremely large ensemble of) expression(s), and stop if e.g. it takes more than `100 ms`. Ergo the hanging of the function `simplify()` is the problem, rather than its output. What's going on behind-the-scenes that's causing sympy to struggle with this expression? Is there a way I can impose a timeout? The Problem is that this is a web application, and queries like `solve(Eq(cos(x)^2+sin(2*x+pi),tan(1)),x)` are able to DDOS searx instances. To solve this we need a clear way to set a a timeout for calculations.

For example, SymPy Gamma has the same issue: http://gamma.sympy.org/input/?i=solve%28Eq%28-sin%282*x%29+%2B+cos%28x%29**2%2Ctan%281%29%29%2C+x%29

This command will add our bot automerge feature to your feedstock. 5. If this PR was opened in error or needs to be updated please add the `bot-rerun` label to this PR. The bot will close this PR and schedule another one. If you do not have permissions to add this label, you can use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerun bot in a PR comment to have the `conda-forge-admin` add it for you. Dependency Analysis -------------------- Please note that this analysis is **highly experimental**. The aim here is to make maintenance easier by inspecting the package's dependencies. Importantly this analysis does not support optional dependencies, please double check those before making changes. If you do not want hinting of this kind ever please add `bot: inspection: disabled` to your `conda-forge.yml`. If you encounter issues with this feature please ping the bot team `conda-forge/bot`. #### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments


#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments

  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments


#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more

notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

* printing
  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

#### References to other Issues or PRs

write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments

  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

#### References to other Issues or PRs

write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments


#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->

* printing
  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments

    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more

  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

#### References to other Issues or PRs

write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

Brought up in opty at https://github.com/csu-hmc/opty/issues/139

This somewhat addresses:

https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/7767

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

In C code generation processes there can be compile errors because some SymPy symbols do not print to valid C variable names. By the time the C code is compiled the error may not be clearly propagated and explained to the user. One option is to tell people up front at code printing time that they have an invalid symbol name. This will at least give a clear error message instead of a possibly obfuscated or hidden compile error.

Maybe the desired solution is to convert a `Symbol.name` into a valid C variable name instead of raising an error. I suppose that people could be printing invalid C code and then fixing it post printing before they compile the C code. If so, this "fix" could break their code. I'll throw this out here to see what people think, but maybe it is not a good solution.

#### Other comments

  * Raise ValueError if Symbol does not print to valid C variable name.

That would also reduce the risk of breaking back-comp.

I recently added a similar printing option in gh-25913. Based upon gh-26395

cc @moorepants @zachetienne 

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
This is a draft for implementing a new printer option for `CodePrinter` which would allow a "fail early" mode for users who are generating code (and for libraries which generates code on the users behalf, and where the user may not be able to decipher compiler error messages at a later stage). That is, an invalid variable name causes `SymPy` to raise an exception already during code-generation. The down-side is that it may trigger false positives, and be overly restrictive for expert use of our code printers, where symbol names often contain extended syntax in the targeted language (e.g. accessing fields in a struct etc.).

#### Other comments
So far this is a draft, it needs, at least:
- [ ] documentation
- [ ] some more test cases

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * CodePrinter got a new option "strict_names" (default is "False") to raise an exception, upon encountering unsupported variable names in a target language.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26227","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26227/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26227/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26227/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26227","id":2132357767,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs5_GTKH","number":26227,"title":"sympify of equation string will only give True or False","user":{"login":"rikardn","id":12795317,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyNzk1MzE3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12795317?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn","html_url":"https://github.com/rikardn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rikardn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-02-13T13:43:43Z","updated_at":"2024-02-14T13:25:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Sympification of a string giving an equation always results in `True` or `False` whereas inequalities will be sympified as inequalities.

```python

import sympy

sympy.sympify("x>y")
>>> x > y

sympy.sympify("x == y")
>>> False
```

I would expect the equality to become `Eq(x, y)`
