was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
In one way, this is a natural extension of gh-25735, there we tested that we could build object files, but there was no natural way to use those in our existing API.


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
A new keyword argument `extra_objs=None` is introduced in a few functions. This is somewhat awkward in `link(obj_files, ...)`, but currently the name of the generated file taken to be the last of the files among `obj_files` by default. This causes problems when, for example, building a Cython extension, since the name of the extension module (shared object file) must match the `PyInit_NAMEOFEXTENSION` symbol for python's loader to work.

#### Other comments
When developing this, I used pytest with the `--pdb` flag, for that workflow `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` has the poor habit of deleting all the interesting files before entering the debugger. Therefore I include a variant here which postpones cleanup to end of process, cf. https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/25735#discussion_r1338913970 .

#### References to other Issues or PRs
In one way, this is a natural extension of gh-25735, there we tested that we could build object files, but there was no natural way to use those in our existing API.


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
A new keyword argument `extra_objs=None` is introduced in a few functions. This is somewhat awkward in `link(obj_files, ...)`, but currently the name of the generated file taken to be the last of the files among `obj_files` by default. This causes problems when, for example, building a Cython extension, since the name of the extension module (shared object file) must match the `PyInit_NAMEOFEXTENSION` symbol for python's loader to work.

#### Other comments
When developing this, I used pytest with the `--pdb` flag, for that workflow `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` has the poor habit of deleting all the interesting files before entering the debugger. Therefore I include a variant here which postpones cleanup to end of process, cf. https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/25735#discussion_r1338913970 .

#### Release Notes

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

#### References to other Issues or PRs
In one way, this is a natural extension of gh-25735, there we tested that we could build object files, but there was no natural way to use those in our existing API.


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
A new keyword argument `extra_objs=None` is introduced in a few functions. This is somewhat awkward in `link(obj_files, ...)`, but currently the name of the generated file taken to be the last of the files among `obj_files` by default. This causes problems when, for example, building a Cython extension, since the name of the extension module (shared object file) must match the `PyInit_NAMEOFEXTENSION` symbol for python's loader to work.

#### Other comments
~~When developing this, I used pytest with the `--pdb` flag, for that workflow `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` has the poor habit of deleting all the interesting files before entering the debugger. Therefore I include a variant here which postpones cleanup to end of process, cf. https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/25735#discussion_r1338913970 .~~

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26090","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26090/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26090/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26090/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26090","id":2086121312,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs58V69g","number":26090,"title":"Overriding functions in lambdify","user":{"login":"Tyrin451","id":223395,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyMzM5NQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/223395?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451","html_url":"https://github.com/Tyrin451","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tyrin451/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-01-17T12:46:44Z","updated_at":"2024-01-18T11:52:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi,

I'm trying to override some functions in lambdify.
The documentation talk about priorization but this doesn't work very well.

Here a working example of my problem :

```python
import numpy as np
import sympy as sm

#some custom func to find max
def cmax(x,y, *args):
    if len(args)>0:
        assert all([isinstance(v, np.ndarray) for v in [x, y, *args]])
        M = np.vstack([ v.ravel() for v in [x, y, *args]])
        return np.amax(M,axis=0)
    elif isinstance(x, np.ndarray) and isinstance(y, np.ndarray):
        return np.where(x>y,x,y)
    elif (isinstance(x, float) or isinstance(x, int))  and isinstance(y, np.ndarray):
        x = x*np.ones(y.shape)
        return np.where(x>y,x,y)
    elif isinstance(x, np.ndarray) and (isinstance(y, float) or isinstance(y, int)):
        y = y*np.ones(x.shape)
        return np.where(x>y,x,y)
    elif (isinstance(x, float) or isinstance(x, int)) and isinstance(y, float) or isinstance(y, int):
        return max(x,y)
    else:
        raise RuntimeError

def create_func(expr,lib):
    symbs = list(expr.free_symbols)
    names = [e.name for e in symbs]
    s = sorted(zip(names,symbs))
    names, symbs = zip(*s)
    return sm.lambdify(symbs,expr, modules=lib), names

#here the expression
expr = sm.sympify('max(a,b)')

# test without explicit call to numpy
func_without_numpy, names = create_func(expr, lib=[{'max':cmax}])

# test with explicit call to numpy
func_with_numpy, names = create_func(expr, lib=[{'max':cmax}, 'numpy'])

print('='*40)
print(f"{func_without_numpy.__doc__}")
print('='*40)
print(f"{func_with_numpy.__doc__}")
print('='*40)

# test value
values = {
    'a' : 4,
    # 'a' : np.array([1,2]),
    'b' : np.array([3,1]),
}

result_without_numpy = func_without_numpy(**values)
print(result_without_numpy)

result_with_numpy = func_with_numpy(**values)
print(result_with_numpy)
```

There is an error with the function created with the numpy module.

I think this behaviour is relatively new because a similar code work well in 2021-2022 (sympy>=1.7.1).

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26090/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26090/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1898335338","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26090#issuecomment-1898335338","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26090","id":1898335338,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs5xJkxq","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-18T11:52:27Z","updated_at":"2024-01-18T11:52:27Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thank you @Tyrin451 for providing a self-contained reproduer, using that I could use `git bisect start --first-parent ...` to identify https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/22169 as the PR which changed the behavior here. That commit got introduced between `sympy-1.9` and `sympy-1.10`.

Note that you spelled `'max'` instead of `'Max'` which is what I would recommend (since that is the name of sympy's Max class). If you capitalize Max, your script does not change behaviour between `sympy-1.9` and `sympy-1.10`.

In general it is hard to know what users expect from sympy's `Max` (or `Min`) when lambdifying. For some applications, `numpy.amax` is better, and for others, `numpy.maximum` is the natural choice. I would encourage you to explicitly use one of those whenever you have expressions which contain these (or any other functionality which has multiple choices with respect possible broadcasting etc., I can't think of other right now but I bet there are more examples, `Piecewise` comes to mind...).

Also, try to use `parse_expr` on strings rather than calling `sympify` on them (the former is more stable and customizeable).

If anyone can come up with a silver bullet to rectify the situation, I'm all ears, but I don't know a simple fix for these issues.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1898335338/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26094","id":2088503430,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs58fAiG","number":26094,"title":"sympify function breaking on numpy datatypes in numpy 2.0.0.dev0","user":{"login":"mironleon","id":119943561,"node_id":"U_kgDOByYxiQ","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/119943561?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon","html_url":"https://github.com/mironleon","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-01-18T15:15:21Z","updated_at":"2024-01-19T09:11:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I could not find if there was already some issue tracking the upcoming release of numpy 2.0, but I was future proofing a project by testing it against the numpy 2 dev release and ran into an issue with `sympify`.

Apologies if you are already working on this

```python
import numpy as np
from sympy.core import sympify

sympify(np.float64(2.34))
```
Will work with numpy 1.x, but it breaks (with the `2.0.0.dev0` numpy) with:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/miron/src/finesse3/test.py", line 4, in <module>
    sympify(np.float64(2.34))
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/sympify.py", line 383, in sympify
    return conv(a)
           ^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/numbers.py", line 1070, in __new__
    num = _convert_numpy_types(num)
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/sympify.py", line 95, in _convert_numpy_types
    return Float(a, precision=prec)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/numbers.py", line 1126, in __new__
    _mpf_ = mlib.from_str(num, precision, rnd)
            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/mpmath/libmp/libmpf.py", line 1331, in from_str
    man, exp = str_to_man_exp(x, base=10)
               ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/mpmath/libmp/libmpf.py", line 1294, in str_to_man_exp
    float(x)
ValueError: could not convert string to float: 'np.float64(2.34)'
```

The issue lies in that numpy seems to have changed their `__repr__` implementation:

numpy 1.x `repr(np.float64(2.34))` returns `'2.34'`, but numpy 2 returns `'np.float64(2.34)'`

This breaks the internal function `_convert_numpy_types` https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/36bcecfeb5b359238a6047c7888555e196d43c89/sympy/core/sympify.py#L76

specifically here:

```python
a = str(list(np.reshape(np.asarray(a), (1, np.size(a)))[0]))[1:-1]
```

because in the end this is calling `str` on a list, which outputs `'[repr(i) for i in list]'`, which in numpy 2 would result in:

`'[np.float64]'` and cutting of the brackets (done with the `[1:-1]` at the end) leaves you with `'np.float64'`, which is eventually gets passed to `float` and fails.

Possible fix would be something like:

```python
# index the resulting list so you are calling `str` directly on the numpy float
a = str(list(np.reshape(np.asarray(a), (1, np.size(a)))[0])[0])[1:-1]
```
Or something similiar.

```python
>>> np.complex128('0.2j') + np.complex128('0.1j') - np.complex128('0.3j')
5.551115123125783e-17j
```
Note that the error is basically epsilon of float64. On my x86-64 machine (which uses 80-bit extended precision for `long double`), I would expect a few more decimal digits to be correct.

I can work around this by constructing `np.float128` for the real and imaginary parts and recombine them afterwards.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
assert abs((np.complex128('0.2j') + np.complex128('0.1j') - np.complex128('0.3j')).imag) <= np.finfo(np.float128).epsilon*0.3
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/26094/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/26094","id":2088503430,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs58fAiG","number":26094,"title":"sympify function breaking on numpy datatypes in numpy 2.0.0.dev0","user":{"login":"mironleon","id":119943561,"node_id":"U_kgDOByYxiQ","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/119943561?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon","html_url":"https://github.com/mironleon","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mironleon/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":13,"created_at":"2024-01-18T15:15:21Z","updated_at":"2024-01-19T13:05:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I could not find if there was already some issue tracking the upcoming release of numpy 2.0, but I was future proofing a project by testing it against the numpy 2 dev release and ran into an issue with `sympify`.

Apologies if you are already working on this

```python
import numpy as np
from sympy.core import sympify

sympify(np.float64(2.34))
```
Will work with numpy 1.x, but it breaks (with the `2.0.0.dev0` numpy) with:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/miron/src/finesse3/test.py", line 4, in <module>
    sympify(np.float64(2.34))
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/sympify.py", line 383, in sympify
    return conv(a)
           ^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/numbers.py", line 1070, in __new__
    num = _convert_numpy_types(num)
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/sympify.py", line 95, in _convert_numpy_types
    return Float(a, precision=prec)
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/sympy/core/numbers.py", line 1126, in __new__
    _mpf_ = mlib.from_str(num, precision, rnd)
            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/mpmath/libmp/libmpf.py", line 1331, in from_str
    man, exp = str_to_man_exp(x, base=10)
               ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "/home/miron/.pyenv/versions/3.11.6/envs/finesse3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/mpmath/libmp/libmpf.py", line 1294, in str_to_man_exp
    float(x)
ValueError: could not convert string to float: 'np.float64(2.34)'
```

The issue lies in that numpy seems to have changed their `__repr__` implementation:

numpy 1.x `repr(np.float64(2.34))` returns `'2.34'`, but numpy 2 returns `'np.float64(2.34)'`

This breaks the internal function `_convert_numpy_types` https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/36bcecfeb5b359238a6047c7888555e196d43c89/sympy/core/sympify.py#L76

specifically here:

```python
a = str(list(np.reshape(np.asarray(a), (1, np.size(a)))[0]))[1:-1]
```

because in the end this is calling `str` on a list, which outputs `'[repr(i) for i in list]'`, which in numpy 2 would result in:

`'[np.float64]'` and cutting of the brackets (done with the `[1:-1]` at the end) leaves you with `'np.float64'`, which is eventually gets passed to `float` and fails.

Possible fix would be something like:

```python
# index the resulting list so you are calling `str` directly on the numpy float
a = str(list(np.reshape(np.asarray(a), (1, np.size(a)))[0])[0])[1:-1]
```
Or something similiar.

```python
>>> np.complex128('0.2j') + np.complex128('0.1j') - np.complex128('0.3j')
5.551115123125783e-17j
```
Note that the error is basically epsilon of float64. On my x86-64 machine (which uses 80-bit extended precision for `long double`), I would expect a few more decimal digits to be correct.

I can work around this by constructing `np.float128` for the real and imaginary parts and recombine them afterwards.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
assert abs((np.complex128('0.2j') + np.complex128('0.1j') - np.complex128('0.3j')).imag) <= np.finfo(np.float128).epsilon*0.3
```

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue
gh-12513

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

#### Reference issue

#### What does this implement/fix?
Avoid infinite loop when using `method="tr-exact"` in `optimize.minimize`.

#### Additional information
I have only a superficial understanding of these methods. So to the best of my knowledge this is an appropriate fix, but someone more familiar with this code and these methods might have another suggestion.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/11027","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/11027/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/11027/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/11027/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/11027","id":149296818,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUxNDkyOTY4MTg=","number":11027,"title":"Should lambdifying Max to numpy use np.amax or np.maximum?","user":{"login":"vperic","id":688281,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4ODI4MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/688281?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic","html_url":"https://github.com/vperic","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/vperic/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":200830774,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMDA4MzA3NzQ=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/utilities.lambdify","name":"utilities.lambdify","color":"0000FF","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2016-04-18T23:05:33Z","updated_at":"2023-11-16T11:17:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When lambdifying `Max` to the `numpy` format, `np.amax` is used. This function returns the maximal element of the passed array. While this is the same as the definition of sympy.Max ("Return, if possible, the maximum value of the list."), it doesn't strike me as very useful. This was added back in 2008 in commit `8f2653b0`. Numpy also has the function `maximum(a,b)`, which returns the element-wise maximum of two passed arrays. This seems like a more useful feature to have.

Should we lambdify to np.maximum instead of np.amax? (the same applies for Min, of course)

Especially tests that highlights any differences in behavior between `amax` which is currently generated and `maximum`.

There have been some pull requests (see e.g. https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/25211) that simply change the code generated but without any written analysis of potential backwards compatiblity (nor any added tests).

Depending on how big the backcomp issue is, we can choose to either:
1. change the emitted code in the next release (if it's very unlikely to break any user's code)
2. have a deprecation cycle, perhaps using some option (e.g. "numpy_min_max=Auto|amin_amax|minimum_maximum"), and have the default be Auto and document in the release notes that the meaning of Auto will change from amin_amax to minimum_maximum in release sympy-1.x.

The exact implementation details of the deprecation cycle (and naming) is just an example, and perhaps its not even needed.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1814245839/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
# Not supported in Python with numpy:
# Derivative
# Derivative
# Derivative
...
```

This PR aims to introduce a printer option "strict" so that those of us who prefer an exception upon codegen (*fail early*) can opt-in to that behavior.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
`CodePrinter._default_settings` got a new entry: `strict=False`.

#### Other comments
Subclasses of `CodePrinter` now "inherits" the entries in `CodePrinter._default_settings`. This way, in the future, printer options may be introduced in `codeprinter.py` without touching the respective language's defaults.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * CodePrinter got a new option "strict" (default is False) to raise an exception instead of generating comments upon encountering unsupported constructs in a target language.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

the n'th time I'm debugging generated code containing:
```
# Not supported in Python with numpy:
# Derivative
# Derivative
# Derivative
...
```

This PR aims to introduce a printer option "strict" so that those of us who prefer an exception upon codegen (*fail early*) can opt-in to that behavior.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact

https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
`CodePrinter._default_settings` got a new entry: `strict=False`.

#### Other comments
Subclasses of `CodePrinter` now "inherits" the entries in `CodePrinter._default_settings`. This way, in the future, printer options may be introduced in `codeprinter.py` without touching the respective language's defaults.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END

  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

CodePrinter","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"For the n'th time I'm debugging generated code containing:
```
# Not supported in Python with numpy:
# Derivative
# Derivative
# Derivative
...
```

This PR aims to introduce a printer option "strict" so that those of us who prefer an exception upon codegen (*fail early*) can opt-in to that behavior.

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->


#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
`CodePrinter._default_settings` got a new entry: `strict=False`.

#### Other comments
Subclasses of `CodePrinter` now "inherits" the entries in `CodePrinter._default_settings`. This way, in the future, printer options may be introduced in `codeprinter.py` without touching the respective language's defaults.

#### Release Notes

<!-- Write the release notes for this release below between the BEGIN and END
statements. The basic format is a bulleted list with the name of the subpackage
and the release note for this PR. For example:

* solvers
  * Added a new solver for logarithmic equations.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

NO ENTRY

See https://github.com/sympy/sympy/wiki/Writing-Release-Notes for more
information on how to write release notes. The bot will check your release
notes automatically to see if they are formatted correctly. -->

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* printing
  * CodePrinter got a new option "strict" (default is generally "True") to raise an exception, instead of generating comments, upon encountering unsupported constructs in a target language.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

import numpy as np
import sympy as sp
from sympy.parsing.latex import parse_latex

expr = parse_latex(r"\ln(x)")
func = sp.lambdify('x', expr, 'numpy')
x_vals = np.linspace(0.1, 10, 400)
y_vals = func(x_vals)
```

```
TypeError: return arrays must be of ArrayType
```

I think it should be doing `np.math.log` where it is currently doing #### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
There was an untested function in `.utilities._compilation`, it used a helper method which we forgot when porting the compilation functionality into sympy.

#### Other comments
cc @moorepants this is where the compilation functionality lives in sympy.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
There was an untested function in `.utilities._compilation`, it used a helper method which we forgot when porting the compilation functionality into sympy.

#### Other comments
cc @moorepants this is where the compilation functionality lives in sympy.

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
There was an untested function in `.utilities._compilation`, it used a helper method which we forgot when porting the compilation functionality into sympy.

#### Other comments
cc @moorepants this is where the compilation functionality lives in sympy.

* functions
  * Fixed a bug with log of integers. Formerly, `log(-x)` incorrectly gave `-log(x)`.

* physics.units
  * Corrected a semantical error in the conversion between volt and statvolt which
    reported the volt as being larger than the statvolt.

or if no release note(s) should be included use:

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
There was an untested function in `.utilities._compilation`, it used a helper method which we forgot when porting the compilation functionality into sympy.

#### Other comments
cc @moorepants this is where the compilation functionality lives in sympy.

Fixes #25438. 

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
`JaxPrinter.printmethod`, which was previously an invalid identifier meaning that custom JAX printing for custom classes couldn't be defined, is now `_jaxcode`.

Is there a way I could configure NumPy or SymPy so that NumPy functions would accept SymPy numbers (e.g. sympy.Rational)?

For example:
```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import sympy
>>> np.linspace(0, sympy.Rational(1,2), 2)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<__array_function__ internals>", line 180, in linspace
  File "C:\Users\idank\mambaforge\lib\site-packages\numpy\core\function_base.py", line 130, in linspace
    dt = result_type(start, stop, float(num))
  File "<__array_function__ internals>", line 180, in result_type
TypeError: Cannot interpret '0.500000000000000' as a data type

>>> np.isnan(sympy.Rational(1,2))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

Background:
I'm using IPython+SymPy+NumPy as a calculator ([details](https://github.com/idanpa/calcpy)).
For convenience, I automatically convert all divisions and floats to sympy.Rational

issues","user":{"login":"brocksam","id":25300795,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MzAwNzk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25300795?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam","html_url":"https://github.com/brocksam","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brocksam/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":84627347,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4NDYyNzM0Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/printing","name":"printing","color":"0000FF","default":false,"description":null},{"id":4990751458,"node_id":"LA_kwDOAAnGFs8AAAABKXjS4g","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/CZI:%20Codegen","name":"CZI: Codegen","color":"0000FF","default":false,"description":"Sam Brockie's CZI-funded postdoc work on codegen and biomechanics"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-07-27T15:17:50Z","updated_at":"2023-07-29T08:34:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Consider the following real example taken from biomechanics, where `fl_T` is the characteristic equation describing the force-length properties of tendon as a function of normalised tendon length `l_T_tilde`:

```python
from sympy import Add, Mul, Symbol, exp
l_T_tilde = Symbol('l_T_tilde')
fl_T1 = 0.2 * exp(33.93669377311689 * (l_T_tilde - 0.995)) - 0.25
# This evaluates to: fl_T1 = 4.32716793402738e-16*exp(33.9366937731169*l_T_tilde) - 0.25
fl_T2 = Add(Mul(0.2, exp(Mul(33.93669377311689, (l_T_tilde - 0.995), evaluate=False)), evaluate=False),
            -0.25, evaluate=False)
# This evaluates to: fl_T2 = 0.2 * exp(33.93669377311689 * (l_T_tilde - 0.995)) - 0.25
```

Normal operating range for `l_T_tilde` is 0.95 < `l_T_tilde` < 1.05. However, there are situations where where a user might need to evaluate the function in the range 0.0 < `l_T_tilde` < 4.0. Higher values would cause overflow for 32 bit single-precision floats. The formulation of `fl_T1` is less well conditioned numerically for values close to the normal operating range, so the formulation of `fl_T2` is preferable for codegen purposes.

Most code printers print `fl_T2` as is. For example, the `C89CodePrinter` will print the desired C code equivalent to `fl_T2`:

```c
0.20000000000000001*exp(33.936693773116893*(l_T_tilde - 0.995)) - 0.25
```

However, the `FCodePrinter` will evaluate the constants inside the `exp` and print undesirable Fortran code more similar to fl_T1: 

```fortran
      0.2d0*2.1635839670136868d-15*exp(33.936693773116893d0*l_T_tilde) -
     @ 0.25d0
```

This is happening with `FCodePrinter._print_Function`. Note that this method is not overloaded for most other printers which is causing the difference in behaviour. https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/db5ca227cb29ca6dcb8da8da6230915f21e927e5/sympy/printing/fortran.py#L303-L311

One option would be amend the implementation of `FCodePrinter._print_Function` to do something sensible when the evaluation of constants results in very large or very small values. Alternative we could leave as is.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25435/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25435/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1656613866","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/25435#issuecomment-1656613866","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25435","id":1656613866,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs5ivevq","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-29T08:34:20Z","updated_at":"2023-07-29T08:34:20Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think we should probably make this aggressive rewriting in the printer opt-in or simply remove it unless we suspect it causes a serious backwards compatibility issue? I suppose the question is if we can find a plausible counter-example where the rewriting is advantageous. But even then, we don't want the Fortran printer to deviate much from, as you point out, the C and C++ printers. From that standpoint I think the current behavior is borderline a bug. And as you have found out, working around this involves explicitly setting `evaluate=False`.

In general I think we should try to move magic rewriting out of the printers and instruct users to preprocess their expressions prior to printing them. This kind of constant folding sounds like it might fit under `sympy.codegen.rewriting`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1656613866/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25420","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25420/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25420/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25420/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/25420","id":1819644825,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs5sdZOZ","number":25420,"title":"[GSoC] Logarithm bases for `\lg` and `\log`","user":{"login":"wermos","id":63574588,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYzNTc0NTg4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/63574588?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos","html_url":"https://github.com/wermos","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wermos/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":19,"created_at":"2023-07-25T06:33:25Z","updated_at":"2023-07-29T08:44:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Background

The ANTLR LaTeX Parser currently parsers `\log` as base `e`, and `\lg` as base 10. While writing the Lark Parser, I wondered how useful this is, because I've only seen `\log` to denote log base 10, and `\lg` as the binary logarithm (i.e., log base 2) in computational complexity-related things.

ANTLR cites ISO 80000-2:2019 as its reference for why `\lg` uses base 10.

When I asked my GSoC mentor @Upabjojr about this, he said that he too believes that `\log` should use base `e`.

### History

Support for `\lg` was added in #23618. In that PR, the base for `\log` was also changed from 10 to $e$, citing the default of the LaTeX printer.

The reasoning given for using `\lg` to mean was

>There seems to be an ISO standard https://en.m.wiktionary.org/wiki/lg#Usage_notes but it’s not widely used in English-language literature. I learned it to be base 10 in China.

But I'm not sure how much weightage we should give to the ISO standards argument, since there is also an [ISO standard about typesetting math which says that symbols like e should be upright](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_31-11#Exponential_and_logarithmic_functions). However, almost no one in mathematics follows that convention.

## Lark Parser

As of right now, I've preserved the ANTLR choice of bases in the Lark rewrite, but once again, I wanted to get the community's thoughts on what base the logarithm should use for both `\log` and `\lg`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25420/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25420/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1656628580","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/25420#issuecomment-1656628580","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25420","id":1656628580,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs5iviVk","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-29T08:44:03Z","updated_at":"2023-07-29T08:44:03Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"In chemistry `log` is base 10 (horrible, I know). I thought it was generally agreed upon that the two letter log abbreviations maps to bases as: `{'lb': 2, 'ln': e, 'lg': 10}`. But I guess the only truly unambiguous form is $\mathrm{log}_x$.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1656628580/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

For example:
```python

>>> np.linspace(0, sympy.Rational(1,2), 2)

    dt = result_type(start, stop, float(num))
  File "<__array_function__ internals>", line 180, in result_type
TypeError: Cannot interpret '0.500000000000000' as a data type

>>> np.isnan(sympy.Rational(1,2))

  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: ufunc 'isnan' not supported for the input types, and the inputs could not be safely coerced to any supported types according to the casting rule ''safe''
```

Background:
I'm using IPython+SymPy+NumPy as a calculator ([details](https://github.com/idanpa/calcpy)).
For convenience, I automatically convert all divisions and floats to sympy.Rational
(and display results both as fractions and as decimals)
After such conversions stuff like np.log(2.33) fails.

(SymPy version 1.12, NumPy version 1.25.1)

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Dummy instances are printed without their unique identifier when formated as string, hence the code emitted by `lambdify` was not valid prior to this fix:
```python
    def _lambdifygenerated(x, y, z):
        _x0 = x + y
        _x1 = -z
>       return [x0_5859678 + z, x0_5859678 + x1_5859679, x1_5859679 + 2*x + 2*y, x0_5859678**2 + (y + z)**2]
E       NameError: name 'x0_5859678' is not defined

<lambdifygenerated-5>:4: NameError
```

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Dummy instances are printed without their unique identifier when formated as string, hence the code emitted by `lambdify` was not valid prior to this fix:
```python
    def _lambdifygenerated(x, y, z):
        _x0 = x + y
        _x1 = -z
>       return [x0_5859678 + z, x0_5859678 + x1_5859679, x1_5859679 + 2*x + 2*y, x0_5859678**2 + (y + z)**2]
E       NameError: name 'x0_5859678' is not defined

<lambdifygenerated-5>:4: NameError
```

#### References to other Issues or PRs
Fixes #24673

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed


#### Other comments
Perhaps this needs to be opt-in with some printer setting for backwards compatibility. (`lambdify` can then use that setting by default). Also, I have found `functools.reduce(operators.add, [...])` to be a better replacement for `sum` in generic python code (but of course I can't remember an example right now).

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
Fixes #10446

Fixes C type declarations so the following is returned:
```
>> ccode(a, declare='double')
'double a'

>> ccode(a**2, b, declare='double')
'double b = pow(a, 2);'
```
Previously, the following was returned:
```
>> ccode(a, declare='double')
Type error!

>> ccode(a**2, b, declare='double')
Type error!

```

I am trying to use `lambdify` to evaluate a sympy expression that contains `tanh`, but I get the following error.

```
TypeError: loop of ufunc does not support argument 0 of type Add which has no callable tanh method
```

The reproducible code and error is this

```python
from sympy import symbols, sympify, tanh
from sympy import lambdify
import json

x, y = symbols("x y")

exp = tanh(x ** 2 + tanh(y)) + y

# this will save the expression in a string in a json file
with open("file.json", "w") as f:
        json.dump(str(exp), f)

with open("file.json", "r") as f:
        loaded_exp = sympify(json.load(f))

# loaded_exp now contains a sympy expression

ld_exp = lambdify(x, loaded_exp) # lambdifying the expression only with respect to x

ld_exp(2) # this line results in the error
```

I think this might be happening because of the tanh function. I am trying to use `lambdify` to evaluate a sympy expression that contains `tanh`, but I get the following error.

```
TypeError: loop of ufunc does not support argument 0 of type Add which has no callable tanh method
```

The reproducible code and error is this

```python
from sympy import symbols, sympify, tanh
from sympy import lambdify
import json

x, y = symbols("x y")

exp = tanh(x ** 2 + tanh(y)) + y

# this will save the expression in a string in a json file
with open("file.json", "w") as f:
        json.dump(str(exp), f)

with open("file.json", "r") as f:
        loaded_exp = sympify(json.load(f))

# loaded_exp now contains a sympy expression

ld_exp = lambdify(x, loaded_exp) # lambdifying the expression only with respect to x

ld_exp(2) # this line results in the error
```

I think this might be happening because of the tanh function. Use milestones to block issues on a release."},{"id":1101649306,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTAxNjQ5MzA2","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/Backwards%20Incompatibility","name":"Backwards Incompatibility","color":"b53917","default":false,"description":"Used to indicate an issue related to a change in the public API."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-05-15T13:34:12Z","updated_at":"2023-05-15T13:34:12Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Every now and then there's talk about fixing some outstanding issues in SymPy's design in a future 2.0 release (e.g. @asmeurer comment [here](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/25103#issuecomment-1533863941))

Personally I think it's fine, and even desirable to completely break backwards compatibility in a 2.0 relase since it allows us to simplify the code base (and we should probably already keep a laundry list of things we intend to fix).

But breaking users code is no small thing, and often research projects don't see updates particularly often, making backwards compatible changes even trickier (breakage go unnoticed until some poor PhD student is tasked with reviving the code). I think that there are two possible remedies:

 - either: the new package is named sympy2 (i.e. pip install sympy2 and `import sympy2`)
 - or: we publish a package on PyPI called `sympy1` (i.e. pip install sympy1) **years before** breaking "sympy".

In both approaches it would allow a single environment to have two different installations of sympy installed in parallel and it would allow the user to install legacy packages requiring the old version of sympy as well as newer packages requiring the latest version of sympy.

The pros/cons are from my perspecitve:
1. The first option require no action among down stream projects (and would get my vote as the preferred option). But puts a burden on future teachability/marketing, and it might be confusing that "pip install sympy" installs a legacy version.
2. The second option avoids having to update tutorials etc. to reference `sympy2` instead of `sympy`, but it requires downstream users of the library to start importing `sympy2` instead of `sympy` well ahead of the breaking release. And this will invariably lead to some breaking of user's code, or require people to set version constraints `<2.0` (which eventually leads to version conflicts, and is of little help in my opinion).

I'm opening this ticket now because I think it would be ideal to give a heads up regardless of what the future approach will be (and my suggested option 2 wold require action long before the actual 2.0 release). Now I'm interested in hearing what you think: do you agree, or do you think version constraints are enough?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

#### References to other Issues or PRs
<!-- If this pull request fixes an issue, write "Fixes #NNNN" in that exact
format, e.g. "Fixes #1234" (see
https://tinyurl.com/auto-closing for more information). Also, please
write a comment on that issue linking back to this pull request once it is
open. -->

See also long discussion in gh-17945

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Adds type hints for various attributes and methods of Basic and Expr. Adds a new `DefinedFunction` class as a subclass of `Function` that should be the base class for defined functions like `sin`, `cos` etc.

These changes are enough for simple end user code involving sympy expressions to be statically analysable so that code completion and editor warnings can work with e.g. the pyright language server that is often used with vscode and other editors (I use pyright from within vim myself now).

It is also possible to use pyright as a type checker e.g.:
```
pip install pyright
pyright sympy
```
(You need to have node and npm installed for this to work.)

Running pyright like this over the codebase takes about 5 minutes on this computer and for master shows:
```
42405 errors, 926 warnings, 0 informations
```
With this PR it is:
```
26598 errors, 39 warnings, 0 informations
```
So this removes around half of all of the "errors" in the codebase and pretty much all the warnings. Most of the other errors are probably not that hard to improve with some type declarations but each case requires a bit of analysis and I thought I would stop here before making the diff any larger.

#### Other comments

It is increasingly common that users of sympy will use an editor like vscode that performs analysis of the code either through type inference or using type hints using a langauge server like pyright. Personally I don't use vscode but I do have the pyright language server running inside vim. The sympy codebase does too many strange things for type inference to work without some type hints. What that means is that if you have pyright running in your editor then just opening a file that uses sympy will cause pyright to consume huge amounts of RAM and CPU while it tries to analyse the sympy codebase. Even then it fails and so the result looks something like this:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1159732/236049488-483bc1c8-bc34-4ff0-a4bf-0ea2ee04b7aa.png)
In other words ordinary code using sympy shows up as being full of errors. The errors are because type inference fails even for basic things like `cos(1)`. It is even worse if you open up a sympy test suite file e.g. `test_manual.py`:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1159732/236054374-a21e7597-508e-4afb-9e86-e4df7796af8b.png)

The number one thing that confuses pyright is `Function.__new__` and I have looked at how to make accurate type hints for `Function.__new__` but it is impossible because it basically works like this:
```python
class Function(Expr):
    def __new__(cls, *args, **kwargs):
        if cls is Function:
            # f = Function('f'), returns an Expr subclass
            return dynamically_created_subclass_of_AppliedUndef
        else:
            # expr = cos(1), returns an Expr instance
            return super().__new__(cls, *args, **kwargs)

class sin(Function):
    # sin is a Function subclass

f = Function('f')  # f is an Expr subclass
```
What this `__new__` method does is that it returns two completely different types of object. More confusingly `Function.__new__` returns a completely different type of object from any `FunctionSubclass.__new__` method. There is just no way to type a method such that it returns a different tpye when called from a subclass.

The only way that I could make sense of this was to introduce a new class:
```python
class Function(Expr):
    def __new__(cls, *args, **kwargs) -> Type[AppliedUndef]:
        if cls is Function:
            # f = Function('f'), returns an Expr subclass
            return dynamically_created_subclass_of_AppliedUndef
        else:
            # Tell the type checker to ignore this. It will only be used by
            # a direct subclass of Function but not by any subclass of 
            # DefinedFunction. We only need to keep this for compatibility.
            return cls._new_(*args, **kwargs)  # type: ignore

    @classmethod
    def _new_(cls, *args, **kwargs) -> Expr:
        return super().__new__(cls, *args, **kwargs)

class DefinedFunction(Function):
    # Override Function.__new__
    def __new__(cls, *args, **kwargs) -> Expr:
        return cls._new_(*args, **kwargs)

class sin(DefinedFunction):
     ...

f = Function('f')  # f is an Expr subclass
```
Most of the lines changed in the diff are just changing many classes to subclass `DefinedFunction` instead of `Function`.

With these changes pyright understands the types of sympy expressions so that it does not report errors and can handle autocompletion etc:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1159732/236052375-f9b73b23-3a77-432f-97d8-82adb97436bd.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1159732/236052616-f2e1f155-cdc3-4a1e-92b7-f46efba76293.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1159732/236053033-98842f46-f4f9-46fb-9d86-bd736393a90f.png)
Obviously this kind of completion/help only works if the language server can infer something about the types of variables like `e2` in the code.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/25145","id":1710118606,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs5l7lbO","number":25145,"title":"Backwards compatibility of future sympy 2.0","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":457124706,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw0NTcxMjQ3MDY=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/Releasing","name":"Releasing","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Issues relating to the automated release scripts. Use milestones to block issues on a release."},{"id":1101649306,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTAxNjQ5MzA2","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/Backwards%20Incompatibility","name":"Backwards Incompatibility","color":"b53917","default":false,"description":"Used to indicate an issue related to a change in the public API."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2023-05-15T13:34:12Z","updated_at":"2023-05-16T15:12:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Every now and then there's talk about fixing some outstanding issues in SymPy's design in a future 2.0 release (e.g. @asmeurer comment [here](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/25103#issuecomment-1533863941))

Personally I think it's fine, and even desirable to completely break backwards compatibility in a 2.0 relase since it allows us to simplify the code base (and we should probably already keep a laundry list of things we intend to fix).

But breaking users code is no small thing, and often research projects don't see updates particularly often, making backwards compatible changes even trickier (breakage go unnoticed until some poor PhD student is tasked with reviving the code). I think that there are two possible remedies:

 - either: the new package is named sympy2 (i.e. pip install sympy2 and `import sympy2`)
 - or: we publish a package on PyPI called `sympy1` (i.e. pip install sympy1) **years before** breaking "sympy".

In both approaches it would allow a single environment to have two different installations of sympy installed in parallel and it would allow the user to install legacy packages requiring the old version of sympy as well as newer packages requiring the latest version of sympy.

The pros/cons are from my perspecitve:
1. The first option require no action among down stream projects (and would get my vote as the preferred option). But puts a burden on future teachability/marketing, and it might be confusing that "pip install sympy" installs a legacy version.
2. The second option avoids having to update tutorials etc. to reference `sympy2` instead of `sympy`, but it requires downstream users of the library to start importing `sympy2` instead of `sympy` well ahead of the breaking release. And this will invariably lead to some breaking of user's code, or require people to set version constraints `<2.0` (which eventually leads to version conflicts, and is of little help in my opinion).

I'm opening this ticket now because I think it would be ideal to give a heads up regardless of what the future approach will be (and my suggested option 2 would require action long before the actual 2.0 release). Now I'm interested in hearing what you think: do you agree, or do you think version constraints are enough?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1549868173","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/25145#issuecomment-1549868173","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25145","id":1549868173,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAnGFs5cYRyN","user":{"login":"bjodah","id":485936,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ4NTkzNg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/485936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah","html_url":"https://github.com/bjodah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjodah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-16T15:12:05Z","updated_at":"2023-05-16T15:12:05Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I did not know that "pymc2" naming scheme was abandoned by that project (looks like there's pymc2 & pymc3, and now with version 4 it's called pymc again).

@asmeurer regarding that this is what version numbers is "designed to solve", I do agree, but that means that some projects might have to put upper constraints (which some consider to be [bad practice](https://iscinumpy.dev/post/bound-version-constraints/)) during a transition period . But if there's a transition period where downstream packages can test "sympy-2.0" behavior (e.g. by setting some environment variable), then perhaps most projects can reassure themselves that they need not specify any such constraints, and those who will need it will have time to get a release out with the constraint set (`sympy<2`).

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25173","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25173/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25173/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25173/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/25173","id":1725996948,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs5m4J-U","number":25173,"title":"Rust codegen is broken","user":{"login":"smups","id":56522208,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTIyMjA4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56522208?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups","html_url":"https://github.com/smups","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/smups/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":327068273,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjcwNjgyNzM=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/codegen","name":"codegen","color":"0000ff","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-05-25T14:59:42Z","updated_at":"2023-05-27T06:27:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The current rust printer is broken, it does not output valid rust code. It sometimes places floats where only integers are allowed and vice versa.

In rust, `2*arg[1].powi(2)` does not compile because 2 is not a float, 2.0 is. `2.0*arg[1].powi(2.0)` also does not compile, because `powi` expects an integer, not a float.

Currently, sympy does not do this correctly. For example:
![afbeelding](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/assets/56522208/8b5079b6-2d7f-4811-8418-666f61d23489)
is printed as:
```rust
2*m.powi(2)*(φ - φ0)*(4*m.powi(4)*φ.powi(3)*(φ - φ0) + ψ0.powi(2)*(φ.powi(2) - 3)*(φ.powi(2) + 1))(φ*(4*m.powi(4)*φ.powi(2)*(φ - φ0).powi(2) + ψ0.powi(2)*(φ.powi(2) + 1).powi(2))*(2*V0 + m.powi(2)*(φ - φ0).powi(2) - 2*ψ*ψ0))
```

I could see someone reaching for [parse_expr](https://docs.sympy.org/latest/modules/parsing.html) when accepting user input in a webapp.

Well, then you better run that webapp in per session isolation:
```pycon
>>> parse_expr("__import__(\"os\").system(\"head -n 1 /etc/passwd\")")                                                                                                                  
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
```
or maybe this one is even more fun:
```pycon
>>> sin("__import__(\"os\").system(\"head -n 1 /etc/passwd\")")                                                                                                                         
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash                                                                                                                                                             
---------------------------------------------------------------------------                                                                                                                 
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last
...
File /usr/local/lib/python3.11/dist-packages/sympy/functions/elementary/trigonometric.py:283, in sin.eval(cls, arg)                                                                         
    281 from sympy.calculus.accumulationbounds import AccumBounds                                                                                                                           
    282 from sympy.sets.setexpr import SetExpr                                                                                                                                              
--> 283 if arg.is_Number:                                                                                                                                                                   
    284     if arg is S.NaN:                                                                                                                                                                
    285         return S.NaN

I would be open for a printer setting to override that setting. Are you assigning in an expression context? (loop conditional perhaps?)

Easiest workaround today would be to override that behavior in a printer subclass:
```pycon
In [68]: class MyCXX(CXX17CodePrinter):                                                                                                                                
    ...:     def _print_Assignment(self, expr):                                                                                                                        
    ...:         return f"{self._print(expr.lhs)} = {self._print(expr.rhs)};"                                                                                          
    ...:                                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                       
In [69]: print(MyCXX().doprint(Assignment(y, Piecewise((0, x<0), (x, True)))))                                                                                         
y = ((x < 0) ? (                                                                                                                                                       
   0                                                                                                                                                                   
)                                                                                                                                                                      
: (                                                                                                                                                                    
   x                                                                                                                                                                   
));
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/comments/1498618207/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25043","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25043/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25043/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/issues/25043/events","html_url":"https://github.com/sympy/sympy/issues/25043","id":1659309937,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAnGFs5i5w9x","number":25043,"title":"Lambdify with cse wrong result","user":{"login":"jokasimr","id":20954731,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwOTU0NzMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20954731?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr","html_url":"https://github.com/jokasimr","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jokasimr/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":200830774,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyMDA4MzA3NzQ=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/sympy/sympy/labels/utilities.lambdify","name":"utilities.lambdify","color":"0000FF","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-04-08T00:24:23Z","updated_at":"2023-04-08T13:44:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Using `lambdify` with `cse=True` sometimes returns the wrong result.
Minimal example:
```python
import sympy as sp
c = sp.symbols("c")
x = sp.symbols("x0", real=True)
poly = c*x**2 + c*(x**4 - x**2)

f = sp.lambdify((c, x), poly, cse=True)  #  <- `cse=True` makes the difference
g = sp.lambdify((c, x), poly, cse=False)

#  wrong result, AssertionError: 256 /= 16
assert f(1, 2) == g(1, 2), f"{f(1, 2)} /= {g(1, 2)}"
```

**Cause**
The issue seems to be related to the filtering of symbols to be excluded in the `tree_cse` function around here [https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/master/sympy/simplify/cse_main.py#L655](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/53a36aa71956c988e2b6ce33db0d8cbcbdfacfbb/sympy/simplify/cse_main.py#L655).

From what I can tell, the cse method aims to avoid name collisions between symbols already in the expression and the symbols representing common subexpressions.
This is done by adding all symbols found in the expression to an `excluded_symbols` set and filtering the `symbols` iterator to exclude them.
However, symbols are equal iff their names and assumptions match, so this approach does not avoid symbol *name collisions*:

```python
from sympy import Symbol
Symbol("x") in {Symbol("x", real=True)}  # returns False
```

**Suggested solution**
In the `tree_cse` function, instead of filtering `symbols` from all symbols occuring in the expression, 
filter from all symbols sharing `name` with some symbol in the expression.
